}} The '''white stork''' ('''''Ciconia ciconia''''') is a large [[bird]] in the [[stork]] family, Ciconiidae. Its [[plumage]] is mainly white, with black on the bird's wings. Adults have long red legs and long pointed red beaks, and measure on average {{cvt|100|–|115|cm}} from beak tip to end of tail, with a {{cvt|155|–|215|cm}} wingspan. The two [[subspecies]], which differ slightly in size, breed in Europe north to [[Finland]], northwestern Africa, [[Palearctic]] east to southern [[Kazakhstan]] and southern Africa. The white stork is a long-distance [[bird migration|migrant]], wintering in Africa from tropical [[Sub-Saharan Africa]] to as far south as [[South Africa]], or on the [[Indian subcontinent]]. When migrating between Europe and Africa, it avoids crossing the [[Mediterranean Sea]] and detours via the [[Levant]] in the east or the [[Strait of Gibraltar]] in the west, because the air [[thermals]] on which it depends for soaring do not form over water. A [[carnivore]], the white stork eats a wide range of animal prey, including insects, fish, amphibians, reptiles, small mammals and small birds. It takes most of its food from the ground, among low vegetation, and from shallow water. It is a [[Monogamy in animals|monogamous]] breeder, and both members of the pair build a large stick nest, which may be used for several years. Each year the female can lay one [[Clutch (eggs)|clutch]] of usually four eggs, which hatch [[wiktionary:asynchronous|asynchronously]] 33–34 days after being laid. Both parents take turns incubating the eggs and both feed the young. The young leave the nest 58–64 days after hatching, and continue to be fed by the parents for a further 7–20 days. The white stork has been rated as [[least concern]] by the [[International Union for Conservation of Nature]] (IUCN). It benefited from human activities during the [[Middle Ages]] as woodland was cleared, but changes in farming methods and industrialisation saw it decline and disappear from parts of Europe in the 19th and early 20th centuries. Conservation and [[reintroduction]] programs across Europe have resulted in the white stork resuming breeding in the Netherlands, Belgium, Switzerland, Sweden and the United Kingdom. It has few natural predators, but may harbour several types of parasite; the plumage is home to [[chewing louse|chewing lice]] and [[feather mite]]s, while the large nests maintain a diverse range of [[mesostigmata|mesostigmatic]] mites. This conspicuous species has given rise to many legends across its range, of which the best-known is the story of babies being brought by storks. == Taxonomy and evolution == English naturalist [[Francis Willughby]] wrote about the white stork in the 17th century, having seen a drawing sent to him by Sir [[Thomas Browne]] of Norwich. He named it ''Ciconia alba''. They noted they were occasional vagrants to England, blown there by storms.{{cite book |last=Willughby |first=Francis |title=The Ornithology of Francis Willughby of Middleton in the County of Warwick, esq. ... |publisher=A.C. for John Martyn |location=London, United Kingdom |date=1681 |page=286 |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/41442085}}{{efn|1=The universally accepted starting point of modern taxonomy for animals is set at 1758, with the publishing of Linnaeus's [[10th edition of Systema Naturae|10th edition of ''Systema Naturae'']], although scientists had been coining names in the previous century.{{cite book |title=Systema Naturae 250 – The Linnaean Ark |first=Andrew |last=Polaszek |publisher=CRC Press |location=Boca Raton, Florida |date=2010 |isbn=978-1-4200-9502-9 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ReWP31_IJSIC&pg=PA34 |page=34}}}} It was one of the many [[Aves in the 10th edition of Systema Naturae|bird species originally described]] by [[Carl Linnaeus]] in the [[10th edition of Systema Naturae|10th edition of ''Systema Naturae'']],{{cite book |last=Linnaeus |first=Carl |author-link=Carl Linnaeus |title=Systema Naturae per Regna Tria Naturae, Secundum Classes, Ordines, Genera, Species, cum Characteribus, Differentiis, Synonymis, Locis |publisher=Laurentii Salvii |location=[[Stockholm|Holmiae]] |volume=I |edition=10th revised |language=la |year=1758 |page=142 |chapter=''Ciconia'' |chapter-url=https://archive.org/stream/carolilinnaeisy00gesegoog#page/n151/mode/1up}} where it was given the binomial name of ''Ardea ciconia''. It was reclassified to and designated the [[type species]] of the new [[genus]] ''[[Ciconia]]'' by [[Mathurin Jacques Brisson]] in 1760.{{cite book |last=Brisson |first=Mathurin Jacques |author-link=Mathurin Jacques Brisson |year=1760 |title=Ornithologie, ou, Méthode Contenant la Division des Oiseaux en Ordres, Sections, Genres, Especes & leurs Variétés |language=fr, la |url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/36010447 |volume=1 |page=48 |place=Paris |publisher=Jean-Baptiste Bauche}}{{cite journal |last=Boles |first=Walter E. |year=2005 |title=A review of the Australian fossil storks of the genus ''Ciconia'' (Aves: Ciconiidae), with the description of a new species |journal=Records of the Australian Museum |volume=57 |issue=2 |pages=165–78 |url=http://australianmuseum.net.au/Uploads/Journals/18014/1440_complete.pdf |doi=10.3853/j.0067-1975.57.2005.1440 |access-date=2011-01-25 |archive-date=2012-09-30 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120930000409/http://australianmuseum.net.au/Uploads/Journals/18014/1440_complete.pdf }} Both the genus and [[specific name (zoology)|specific epithet]], ''cĭcōnia'', are the [[Latin]] word for "stork".{{cite book |title=An Elementary Latin Dictionary |last1=Lewis |first1=Charlton Thomas |last2=Kingery |first2=Hugh Macmaster |location=New York |publisher=American Book Company |year=1918 |page=126 |url=https://archive.org/details/bub_gb_2ncKAAAAIAAJ/page/n132 |isbn=978-0-19-910205-1}} [[File:Ciconia_alba_119.jpg|thumb|left|upright|Skeleton]] There are two [[subspecies]]: * ''C. c. ciconia'', the [[Subspecies#Nominate subspecies|nominate subspecies]] described by Linnaeus in 1758, breeds from Europe to northwestern Africa and westernmost Asia and in southern Africa, and winters mainly in Africa south of the [[Sahara Desert]],{{sfn|Elliott|1992|pp=460-1}} though some birds winter in India.{{cite book |title=Handbook of the Birds of India and Pakistan |volume=1 |last1=Ali |first1=Salim |last2=Ripley |first2=Dillon S. |year=2001 |edition=2nd |type=paperback |publisher=[[Oxford University Press]] |location=India |isbn= 978-0-19-565934-4 |pages=99–101}} * ''C. c. asiatica'', described by Russian naturalist [[Nikolai Severtzov]] in 1873, breeds in [[Turkestan]] and winters from Iran to India. It is slightly larger than the nominate subspecies.{{sfn|Elliott|1992|pp=460-1}}{{cite book |title=Birds of South Asia: The Ripley Guide |volume=2 |last1=Rasmussen |first1=Pamela C. |author-link1=Pamela C. Rasmussen |last2=Anderton |first2=John C. |year=2005 |location=Washington, DC; Barcelona |publisher=Smithsonian Institution and Lynx Edicions |isbn=978-84-87334-66-5 |page=63}} The stork family contains six genera in three broad groups: the open-billed and wood storks (''[[Mycteria]]'' and ''[[Anastomus]]''), the giant storks (''[[Ephippiorhynchus]]'', ''[[Jabiru]]'' and ''[[Leptoptilos]]'') and the "typical" storks (''[[Ciconia]]''). The typical storks include the white stork and six other [[Extant taxon|extant]] species,{{sfn|Elliott|1992|p=437}} which are characterised by straight pointed beaks and mainly black and white plumage.{{cite journal |last=Kahl |first=M. Philip |year=1987| title=An overview of the storks of the world | journal=Colonial Waterbirds |volume=10 |issue=2 |pages=131–34 |doi=10.2307/1521251 |jstor=1521251}} Its closest relatives are the larger, black-billed [[Oriental stork]] (''Ciconia boyciana'') of [[East Asia]], which was formerly classified as a subspecies of the white stork,{{sfn|Elliott|1992|pp=460-1}} and the [[maguari stork]] (''C. maguari'') of South America. Close evolutionary relationships within ''Ciconia'' are suggested by behavioural similarities and, biochemically, through analysis of both mitochondrial [[cytochrome b|cytochrome ''b'']] gene sequences and [[DNA–DNA hybridization]].{{cite journal |last=Slikas |first=Beth |year=1997 |title=Phylogeny of the avian family Ciconiidae (storks) based on cytochrome ''b'' Sequences and DNA–DNA hybridization distances |journal=Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution |volume=8 |issue=3 |pages=275–300 |doi=10.1006/mpev.1997.0431 |pmid=9417889 |doi-access=free|bibcode=1997MolPE...8..275S }} A ''Ciconia'' fossil representing the [[Anatomical terms of location#Proximal and distal|distal]] end of a right [[humerus]] has been recovered from [[Miocene]] beds of [[Rusinga Island]], Lake Victoria, Kenya. The 24–6 million year old fossil could have originated from either a white stork or a [[black stork]] (''C. nigra''), which are species of about the same size with very similar bone structures. The [[Middle Miocene]] beds of [[Maboko Island]] have yielded further remains.{{cite journal |year=2008 |title=New records of fossil 'waterbirds' from the Miocene of Kenya |journal=[[American Museum Novitates]] |issue=3610 |hdl=2246/5906 |last1=Dykes |first1=Gareth J. |last2=Alexander |first2=Cyril Walker|hdl-access=free}} == Description == [[File:White Stork.jpg|thumb|A juvenile feeding on an insect]] The white stork is a large bird. It has a length of {{convert|100|–|115|cm|in|abbr=on}},{{efn|1=By convention, length is measured from the tip of the bill to the tip of the tail on a dead bird (or skin) laid on its back.}}{{sfn|Cramp|1977|p=3}} and a standing height of {{convert|100|-|125|cm|in|abbr=on}}. The wingspan is {{convert|155|–|215|cm|in|abbr=on}} and its weight is {{convert|2.3|–|4.5|kg|lb|abbr=on}}.{{cite book |last1= Mead |first1= C. |last2= Ogilvie |first2= M. |year= 2007 |title= The Atlas of Bird Migrations: Tracing the Great Journeys of the World's Birds |location=Cape Town |publisher=Struik |pages=88–89 |isbn=978-1-77007-499-6}}{{cite book |last1= Hancock|first1=James |last2= Kushan|first2=James A. |title= Storks, Ibises and Spoonbills of the World |publisher= [[Princeton University Press]] |year= 1992 |isbn= 978-0-12-322730-0 |chapter=White stork}} Like all storks, it has long legs, a long neck and a long straight pointed [[beak]].{{sfn|Elliott|1992|p=437}} The sexes are identical in appearance, except that males are larger than females on average.{{sfn|Elliott|1992|pp=460-1}} The plumage is mainly white with black [[flight feather]]s and wing [[covert (feather)|coverts]]; the black is caused by the pigment [[melanin]].{{cite journal |last1= Grande |first1= Juan Manuel |last2= Negro |first2= Juan José |last3= María Torres |first3= José | year= 2004| title= The evolution of bird plumage colouration; A role for feather-degrading bacteria? | journal= Ardeola | volume= 51 | issue = 2 | pages= 375–83 |url =http://www.ardeola.org/files/1198.pdf |doi= 10.1007/s00114-008-0462-0|pmid= 18853129 |s2cid= 12283403 }} The breast feathers are long and shaggy forming a ruff which is used in some courtship displays.{{sfn|Cramp|1977|p=335}} The [[iris (anatomy)|irises]] are dull brown or grey, and the peri-[[orbit (anatomy)|orbital]] skin is black. The adult has a bright red beak and red legs,{{sfn|Elliott|1992|pp=460-1}} the colouration of which is derived from [[carotenoid]]s in the diet. In parts of Spain, studies have shown that the pigment is based on [[astaxanthin]] obtained from an introduced species of crayfish (''[[Procambarus clarkii]]'') and the bright red beak colours show up even in nestlings, in contrast to the duller beaks of young white storks elsewhere.{{cite journal |journal= Comparative Biochemistry and Physiology B |volume= 126 |issue= 3 |year= 2000| pages= 347–52 |title= Astaxanthin is the major carotenoid in tissues of White Storks (''Ciconia ciconia'') feeding on introduced Crayfish (''Procambarus clarkii'') |last1= Negro |first1= Juan José |last2= Garrido-Fernandez |first2= Juan |doi= 10.1016/S0305-0491(00)00180-2|pmid=11007176}} [[File:Ciconia ciconia 112916256, crop.jpg|thumb|left|In flight. White storks fly with their necks outstretched.]] As with other storks, the wings are long and broad, enabling the bird to soar.{{sfn|Elliott|1992|p=438}} In flapping [[bird flight|flight]] its wingbeats are slow and regular. It flies with its neck stretched forward and with its long legs extended well beyond the end of its short tail. It walks at a slow and steady pace with its neck upstretched. In contrast, it often hunches its head between its shoulders when resting.{{sfn|Cramp|1977|p=328}} [[Moulting#Birds|Moulting]] has not been extensively studied, but appears to take place throughout the year, with the primary flight feathers replaced over the breeding season.{{sfn|Cramp|1977|p=335}} [[File:Ciconia ciconia -Vogelpark Avifauna, The Netherlands -juvenile-8a.jpg|thumb|right|upright|alt=Head, neck and upper body of a white stork with a long beak with is reddish at the base fading to black at the tip|An older juvenile at [[Vogelpark Avifauna]], Netherlands. Beaks turn red starting at the base.]] Upon hatching, the young white stork is partly covered with short, sparse, whitish [[down feather]]s. This early down is replaced about a week later with a denser coat of woolly white down. By three weeks, the young bird acquires black scapulars and [[flight feather]]s. On hatching, the chick has pinkish legs, which turn to greyish-black as it ages. Its beak is black with a brownish tip.{{sfn|Cramp|1977|p=335}} By the time it [[fledge]]s, the juvenile bird's plumage is similar to that of the adult, though its black feathers are often tinged with brown, and its beak and legs are a duller brownish-red or orange. The beak is typically orange or red with a darker tip.{{sfn|Cramp|1977|p=328}} The bills gain the adults' red colour the following summer, although the black tips persist in some individuals. Young storks adopt adult plumage by their second summer.{{sfn|Van den Bossche|2002|p=11}} === Similar species === Within its range, the white stork is distinctive when seen on the ground. The winter range of ''C. c. asiatica'' overlaps that of the [[Asian openbill]], which has similar plumage but a different bill shape. When seen at a distance in flight, the white stork can be confused with several other species with similar underwing patterns, such as the [[yellow-billed stork]], [[great white pelican]] and [[Egyptian vulture]].{{sfn|Cramp|1977|p=328}} The yellow-billed stork is identified by its black tail and a longer, slightly curved, yellow beak. The white stork also tends to be larger than the yellow-billed stork. The great white pelican has short legs that do not extend beyond its tail, and it flies with its neck retracted, keeping its head near to its stocky body, giving it a different flight profile.{{sfn|Cramp|1977|p=228}} Pelicans also behave differently, soaring in orderly, synchronised flocks rather than in disorganised groups of individuals as the white stork does.{{sfn|Svensson|Grant|1999|p=23}} The Egyptian vulture is much smaller, with a long wedge-shaped tail, shorter legs and a small yellow-tinged head on a short neck.{{sfn|Svensson|Grant|1999|p=74}} The [[common crane]], which can also look black and white in strong light, shows longer legs and a longer neck in flight.{{sfn|Svensson|Grant|1999|p=34}} == Distribution and habitat == [[File:Stork 1000798.JPG|left|thumb|A flock foraging in Turkey. White storks avoid areas overgrown with tall grass and shrubs.|alt=Several black and white birds with long red legs and long red beaks walk in a green grassy area.]] The [[nominate race]] of the white stork has a wide although disjunct summer range across Europe, clustered in the [[Iberian Peninsula]] and North Africa in the west, and much of eastern and central Europe, with 25% of the world's population concentrated in Poland,{{cite journal |last1= Chernetsov |year= 2006 |title= Sex-related natal dispersal of White Storks (''Ciconia ciconia'') in Poland: How far and where to? |journal= The Auk |volume= 123 |issue= 4 |pages= 1103–9 |url= http://www.zin.ru/rybachy/chernetsov-pdf/PDF21a.pdf |doi= 10.1642/0004-8038(2006)123[1103:SNDOWS]2.0.CO;2 |first1= Nikita |last2= Chromik |first2= Wiesław |last3= Dolata |first3= Pawel T. |last4= Profus |first4= Piotr |last5= Tryjanowski |first5= Piotr |s2cid= 84227358 |display-authors= 4 |via= The [[Russian Academy of Sciences]] Zoological Institute: Biological Station Rybachy}} as well as parts of [[western Asia]]. The ''asiatica'' population of about 1450 birds is restricted to a region in central Asia between the [[Aral Sea]] and [[Xinjiang]] in western China.{{sfn|Van den Bossche|2002|p=10}}{{cite journal |volume= 4 |url= https://archive.org/stream/strayfeathersjou41876hume#page/99/mode/1up |year= 1876 |title= A contribution to the ornithology of eastern Turkestan |last1= Scully |first1= John |journal= Stray Feathers |pages= 41–205 |via= The [[Internet Archive]]}} The Xinjiang population is believed to have become extinct around 1980.{{cite book |last1= Ma |first1= Ming |last2= Dai |first2= Cai |year= 2002 |chapter= The Fate of the White Stork (''Ciconia ciconia asiatica'') in Xinjiang, China |title=Abstract Volume. 23rd International Ornithological Congress, Beijing, August 11–17, 2002 |page= 352}} [[bird migration|Migration]] routes extend the range of this species into many parts of Africa and India. Some populations adhere to the eastern migration route, which passes across Israel into eastern and central Africa.{{cite journal |title= Detection of a new important staging and wintering area of the White Stork ''Ciconia ciconia'' by satellite tracking |last1= Berthold |first1= Peter |last2= Van Den Bossche |first2= Willem |last3= Fiedler |first3= Wolfgang |last4= Kaatz |first4= Christoph |last5= Kaatz |first5= Michael |last6= Leshem |first6= Yossi |last7= Nowak |first7= Eugeniusz |last8= Querner |first8= Ulrich |display-authors= 4 |journal= Ibis |volume= 143 |issue= 4 |pages= 450–5 |year= 2001 |doi= 10.1111/j.1474-919X.2001.tb04946.x |url= http://www.tau.ac.il/lifesci/departments/zoology/members/leshem/documents/19.pdf |via= [[Tel Aviv University]]|citeseerx= 10.1.1.579.7034 }}{{cite journal |url= http://www.tau.ac.il/lifesci/zoology/members/yom-tov/articles/The_effect_of_wind.pdf |last1= Shamoun-Baranes |first1= Judy |last2= Baharad |first2= Anat |last3= Alpert |first3= Pinhas |last4= Berthold |first4= Peter |last5= Yom-Tov |first5= Yoram |last6= Dvir |first6= Yoav |last7= Leshem |first7= Yossi |display-authors= 4 |title= The effect of wind, season and latitude on the migration speed of White Storks ''Ciconia ciconia'', along the eastern migration route |journal= Journal of Avian Biology |volume= 34 |pages= 97–104 |year= 2003 |doi= 10.1034/j.1600-048X.2003.03079.x |via= [[Tel Aviv University]]}} In Africa the white stork may spend the winter in Tunisia, Morocco, Uganda, Angola, Zimbabwe, Djibouti, Botswana, Mozambique, Zambia, Eswatini, Gambia, Guinea, Algeria, and Ghana. A few records of breeding from South Africa have been known since 1933 at [[Calitzdorp]], and about 10 birds have been known to breed since the 1990s around [[Bredasdorp]].{{cite book |pages= 82–3 |title= The Atlas of Southern African Birds |volume=1: Non-passerines |last= Allan |first= David G. |year= 1997 |chapter= White Stork |editor-last1= Harrison |editor-first1= J.A. |editor-last2= Allan |editor-first2= D.G. |editor-last3= Underhill |editor-first3= L.G. |editor-last4= Herremans |editor-first4= M. |editor-last5= Tree |editor-first5= A.J. |editor-last6=Parker |editor-first6=V. |editor-last7= Brown |editor-first7= C.J. |publisher= BirdLife South Africa |isbn= 978-0-620-20729-4 |chapter-url= http://sabap2.adu.org.za/docs/sabap1/083.pdf |via= South African Bird Atlas Project 2}} A small population of white storks winters in India and is thought to derive principally from the ''C. c. asiatica'' population as flocks of up to 200 birds have been observed on spring migration in the early 1900s through the [[Kurram Valley]].{{cite journal |last1= Whitehead |first1= Charles Hughes Tempest |year= 1911 |title= On the birds of Kohat and the Kurram Valley. Northern India. Part 3 |pages= 954–80 |volume= 20 |issue= 4 |journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/30167241}} However, birds [[bird ringing|ringed]] in Germany have been recovered in western ([[Bikaner]]) and southern ([[Tirunelveli district|Tirunelveli]]) India.{{cite journal |last1= Prater |first1= Stanley Henry |author-link1= Stanley Henry Prater |year= 1931 |title= The migration of the White Stork ''Ciconia ciconia'' |journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume= 35 |issue= 2 |page= 459|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/47540483}} An atypical specimen with red orbital skin, a feature of the Oriental white stork, has been recorded{{cite journal |last1= Jadeja |first1= Vijayraj |last2= Vyas |first2= Raju |last3= Parasharya |first3= B.M. |year= 1998 |title= Record of ''Ciconia ciconia asiaticus'' from Indian Territory |journal= Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume= 95 |issue= 1 |page= 113|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48604633}} and further study of the Indian population is required. North of the breeding range, it is a passage migrant or [[Vagrancy (biology)|vagrant]] in Finland, Iceland, Ireland, Norway and Sweden, and west to the [[Azores]] and [[Madeira]]. Despite their geographical proximity, in Finland the species is rare, while in Estonia there are an estimated 5,000 breeding pairs.{{cite news |title=Tammitikka, viitatiainen ja kattohaikara ovat Virossa tuttuja, Suomessa harvinaisuuksia – linturetkeily Suomesta Viroon kasvattaa taas suosiotaan |url=https://yle.fi/uutiset/3-12593227 |access-date=4 September 2022 |publisher=Yle |date=4 September 2022 |language=fi}} In recent years, the range has expanded into western Russia.{{sfn|Snow|Perrins|Gillmor|etal|1998|p=141–143}}{{cite book |last1= Thomsen |first1= Kai-Michael |last2= Hötker |first2= Hermann |year= 2006 |chapter= The Sixth International White Stork Census: 2004–2005 |title= Waterbirds Around the World |editor-last1= Boere |editor-first1= G.C. |editor-last2= Galbraith |editor-first2= C.A. |editor-last3= Stroud |editor-first3=D.A. |publisher= [[The Stationery Office]] |place=Edinburgh |pages= 493–5 |chapter-url= http://www.jncc.gov.uk/PDF/pub07_waterbirds_part4.3.18.pdf |url= http://jncc.defra.gov.uk/worldwaterbirds |isbn= 978-0-11-497333-9}} The white stork's preferred feeding grounds are grassy meadows, farmland and shallow wetlands. It avoids areas overgrown with tall grass and shrubs.{{cite journal |last1= Johst |first1= Karin |last2= Brandl |first2= Rolan |last3= Pfeifer |first3= Robert |year= 2001 |title= Foraging in a patchy and dynamic landscape: Human land use and the White Stork |journal = Ecological Applications | volume= 11 |pages= 60–9 |doi= 10.1890/1051-0761(2001)011[0060:FIAPAD]2.0.CO;2|issn=1051-0761}} In the [[Chernobyl (city)|Chernobyl]] area of northern Ukraine, white stork populations declined after the [[Chernobyl disaster|1986 nuclear accident]], as farmland was [[ecological succession|succeeded]] by tall grass and shrubs.{{cite journal |last1= Samusenko |first1= Irina |year= 2004 |title= Some aspects of White Stork ''Ciconia ciconia'' population dynamics in the region of Chernobyl's accident |journal= Bird Census News |volume= 13 |issue= 2000 |pages= 157–60 |url= http://www.ebcc.info/wpimages/other/18-SamusenkaH.pdf |access-date= 2010-12-03 |archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20110627090520/http://www.ebcc.info/wpimages/other/18-SamusenkaH.pdf |archive-date= 2011-06-27 }} In parts of Poland, poor natural foraging grounds have forced birds to seek food at [[landfill|rubbish dump]]s since 1999.{{cite journal |journal= European Journal of Wildlife Research |volume= 56 |issue= 1 |pages= 83–7 |doi= 10.1007/s10344-009-0313-0 |title= White Storks, ''Ciconia ciconia'', forage on rubbish dumps in Poland — A novel behaviour in population |last1= Kruszyk |first1= Robert |last2= Ciach |first2= Michał | year= 2010|bibcode= 2010EJWR...56...83K |s2cid= 37147808 |url= https://hal.archives-ouvertes.fr/hal-00535245/file/10344_2009_313_ReferencePDF.pdf }} White storks have also been reported foraging in rubbish dumps in the Middle East, North Africa and South Africa.{{cite journal |journal= Waterbirds |title= Foraging of White Storks ''Ciconia ciconia'' on rubbish dumps on non-breeding grounds |last1= Ciach |first1= Michał |last2= Kruszyk |first2= Robert |volume= 33 |issue= 1 |pages= 101–4 |year= 2010 |doi= 10.1675/063.033.0112|s2cid= 85910168 }} Anthropogenic litter was found in the pellets of one third of breeding pairs in Poland, even though all pairs nested far from major dumps and landfills.{{Cite journal |last1=Mikula |first1=Peter |last2=Karg |first2=Jerzy |last3=Jerzak |first3=Leszek |last4=Walasz |first4=Kazimierz |last5=Siekiera |first5=Joachim |last6=Czyż |first6=Stanisław |last7=Mikicińska |first7=Katarzyna |last8=Pietkiewicz |first8=Małgorzata |last9=Sztwiertnia |first9=Hanna |last10=Wyka |first10=Jakub |last11=Tryjanowski |first11=Piotr |date=2023-12-29 |title=Diet analysis and the assessment of plastic and other indigestible anthropogenic litter in the white stork pellets |url=https://link.springer.com/10.1007/s11356-023-31710-2 |journal=Environmental Science and Pollution Research |language=en |volume=31 |issue=5 |pages=6922–6928 |doi=10.1007/s11356-023-31710-2 |pmid=38157174 |bibcode= |s2cid=266653719 |issn=1614-7499|url-access=subscription }} The white stork breeds in greater numbers in areas with open grasslands, particularly grassy areas which are wet or periodically flooded, and less in areas with taller vegetation cover such as forest and shrubland.{{cite journal |last1= Carrascal |first1= Luis María |last2= Bautista |first2= Luis Miguel |last3= Lázaro |first3= Encarnación |year= 1993 |title= Geographical variation in the density of the White Stork ''Ciconia ciconia'' in Spain: Influence of habitat structure and climate |journal= Biological Conservation |volume= 65 |issue= 1 |pages= 83–87 |url=https://digital.csic.es/bitstream/10261/51857/6/51857_posprint_LuisMBautista.pdf |hdl=10261/51857 |hdl-access=free |doi= 10.1016/0006-3207(93)90200-K|bibcode= 1993BCons..65...83C |s2cid= 67831806 }} They make use of grasslands, wetlands, and farmland on the wintering grounds in Africa. White storks were probably aided by human activities during the [[Middle Ages]] as woodland was cleared and new pastures and farmland were created, and they were found across much of Europe, breeding as far north as Sweden. The population in Sweden is believed to have been established in the 16th century, following the clearing of forests for agricultural purposes. Approximately 5,000 pairs were estimated to breed in the 18th century, but this number subsequently declined. The first accurate census in 1917 found 25 pairs, and the last pair failed to breed around 1955.{{cite journal |last1= Cavallin |first1= Berith |year= 1997 | title= Storklandskapet |journal= Skånes Natur |volume= 84 |pages= 1–160 |language= sv}} A similar pattern was seen in Denmark where the white stork appears to have become established in the 15th century when forests were being replaced by farmland and meadows, followed by a rapid population increase in the next centuries and then a rapid decline due mainly to modern, high-intensity agriculture in the last 200 years.{{cite journal | last=Thellesen | first=P.V. | year=2020 | title=Ynglebestanden af Hvid Stork i Danmark 1900-2018 med et historisk tilbageblik | journal=Dansk Orn. Foren. Tidsskr. | volume=114 | pages=33–41 }} The white stork has been a rare visitor to the [[British Isles]], with about 20 birds seen in Britain every year, and prior to 2020 there were no records of nesting since a pair nested atop [[St Giles' Cathedral|St Giles High Kirk]] in [[Edinburgh]], Scotland, in 1416.{{cite book |page= 80 |url= https://archive.org/stream/cu31924005489558#page/n88/mode/1up |title= Early Annals of Ornithology |last= Gurney |first= John Henry |year= 1921 |publisher= H F & G Witherby |location= London |via= The [[Internet Archive]]}} In 2020, a pair bred in the United Kingdom for the first time in over 600 years,{{cite news |last1=Elassar |first1=Alaa |title=Wild white storks hatched in the UK for the first time in centuries |url=https://edition.cnn.com/2020/05/16/uk/wild-white-storks-hatched-uk-trnd/index.html |access-date=18 May 2020 |publisher=CNN |date=16 May 2020}} as part of a re-introduction initiative in [[West Sussex]] called the White Stork Project.{{cite news |title=Wild white storks hatch in UK for first time in hundreds of years |url=https://www.theguardian.com/environment/2020/may/16/wild-white-storks-hatch-for-first-time-in-hundreds-of-years |access-date=18 May 2020 |work=The Guardian |date=16 May 2020}} A decline in population began in the 19th century due to [[industrialisation]] and changes in agricultural methods. White storks no longer nest in many countries, and the current strongholds of the western population are in Portugal, Spain, Ukraine and Poland. In the Iberian Peninsula, populations are concentrated in the southwest, and have also declined due to agricultural practices. A study published in 2005 found that the [[Podhale]] region in the uplands of southern Poland had seen an influx of white storks, which first bred there in 1931 and have nested at progressively higher altitudes since, reaching 890 m (3000 ft) in 1999. The authors proposed that this was related to [[Global warming|climate warming]] and the influx of other animals and plants to higher altitudes.{{cite journal |last1= Tryjanowski |first1= Piotr |last2= Sparks |first2= Tim H. |last3= Profus |first3= Piotr |year= 2005 |title= Uphill shifts in the distribution of the White Stork ''Ciconia ciconia'' in southern Poland: The importance of nest quality |journal= Diversity and Distributions |volume= 11 |issue= 3 |pages=219–23 |doi= 10.1111/j.1366-9516.2005.00140.x|bibcode= 2005DivDi..11..219T |s2cid= 86013333 }} White storks arriving in Poznań province ([[Greater Poland Voivodeship]]) in western Poland in spring to breed did so some 10 days earlier in the last twenty years of the 20th century than at the end of the 19th century.{{cite journal |last1= Ptaszyk |year= 2003 |title= Changes in the timing and pattern of arrival of the White Stork (''Ciconia ciconia'') in western Poland |journal= Journal für Ornithologie |volume= 144 |issue= 3 |pages= 323–9 |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1046/j.1439-0361.2003.03011.x |doi= 10.1007/BF02465632 |first1= J. |last2= Kosicki |first2= J. |last3= Sparks |first3= T.H. |last4= Tryjanowski |first4=P. |bibcode= 2003JOrni.144..323P |s2cid= 40392092 |url-access= subscription }} === Migration === [[File:Rostocker Pfeilstorch.jpg|thumb|upright|In 1822, the ''Rostocker [[Pfeilstorch]]'' provided early evidence of long-distance stork migration.]] [[File:Whitestorkflock.jpg|thumb|A flock in migration over Israel. Migrating white storks use the uplift of air [[thermal]]s to reduce the effort of long-distance flying.|alt=A blue sky with many tiny silhouettes of distant flying birds]] Systematic research into migration of the white stork began with [[Germany|German]] ornithologist [[Johannes Thienemann]] who commenced [[bird ringing]] studies in 1906 at the [[Rossitten Bird Observatory]], on the [[Curonian Spit]] in what was then [[East Prussia]]. Although not many storks passed through Rossitten itself, the observatory coordinated the large-scale ringing of the species throughout Germany and elsewhere in Europe. Between 1906 and the [[World War II|Second World War]], about 100,000, mainly juvenile, white storks were ringed, with over 2,000 long-distance recoveries of birds wearing Rossitten rings reported between 1908 and 1954.{{cite journal |last1= Sproll |first1= Alexandra |last2= Fiedler |first2= Wolfgang |year= 2001 |title= Digging in old data: Migration and causes of death in White Storks (''Ciconia ciconia'') according to ringing recovery data of the Vogelwarte Rossitten (Eastern Prussia) before the second World War |journal= Euring Newsletter |volume= 3 |url= http://www.euring.org/about_euring/newsletter3/sproll.htm |publisher= European Union for Bird Ringing |archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20110722022620/http://www.euring.org/about_euring/newsletter3/sproll.htm |archive-date= 2011-07-22 }} ==== Routes ==== White storks fly south from their summer breeding grounds in Europe in August and September, heading for Africa. There, they spend the winter in [[savannah]] from Kenya and Uganda south to the [[Cape Province]] of South Africa.{{sfn|Cramp|1977|p=331}} In these areas, they congregate in large flocks which may exceed a thousand individuals.{{sfn|Cramp|1977|p=328}} Some diverge westwards into western Sudan and Chad, and may reach Nigeria. In spring, the birds return north; they are recorded from Sudan and Egypt from February to April.{{cite journal |last1=Reed |year=1969 |title= The migration of the White Stork in Egypt and adjacent areas |journal= The Condor |url= http://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/condor/v071n02/p0146-p0154.pdf |volume= 71 |issue= 2 |pages= 146–54 |doi= 10.2307/1366076 |first1= C.A. |last2= Lovejoy |first2= T.E. |via= [[University of New Mexico]]: Searchable Ornithological Research Archive|jstor= 1366076}} They arrive back in Europe around late March and April, after an average journey of 49 days. By comparison, the autumn journey is completed in about 26 days. [[Tailwind]]s and scarcity of food and water en route (birds fly faster over regions lacking resources) increase average speed. [[File:2017-03-11 Migratory storks resting in Rahat.jpg|thumb|Eastern route: storks resting in [[Rahat]] (spring 2017)]] To avoid a long sea crossing over the Mediterranean, birds from central Europe either follow an eastern migration route by crossing the [[Bosphorus]] in Turkey, traversing the [[Levant]], then bypassing the [[Sahara Desert]] by following the [[Nile]] valley southwards, or follow a western route over the [[Strait of Gibraltar]].{{cite journal |doi= 10.1111/j.1474-919X.1998.tb04539.x |journal= Ibis |volume= 140 |pages= 41–52 |year= 1998 |title= Routes of migrating soaring birds |last1= Leshem |first1= Yossi |last2= Yom-Tov |first2= Yoram |url= http://www.tau.ac.il/lifesci/departments/zoology/members/leshem/documents/17.pdf |via= [[Tel Aviv University]]}} These migration corridors maximise help from the thermals and thus save energy.{{cite book |last= Alexander |first= R. McNeill |year= 1992 |title= Exploring Biomechanics, Animals in Motion |location= New York |publisher= Scientific American Library |isbn= 978-0-7167-5035-2 |url-access= registration |url= https://archive.org/details/exploringbiomech00alex}}{{cite journal|title=Flight behaviour of White Storks ''Ciconia ciconia'' on their migration over southern Israel |last1= Liechti |first1= Felix |last2= Ehrich |first2= Dorothee |last3= Bruderer |first3= Bruno |year= 1996 |journal= Ardea |volume =84 |pages= 3–13 |url=http://www.avibirds.com/pdf/O/Ooievaar8.pdf}} In winter 2013–2014, white storks were observed in southern India's [[Mudumalai National Park]] for the first time.{{cite journal |author1=Samson, A. |name-list-style=amp |author2=Ramakrishnan, B. |year=2018 |title=White Stork: Sighting record of ''Ciconia ciconia'' (Linnaeus, 1758) in Mudumalai Tiger Reserve, Nilgiris, India |journal=Zoo's Print |volume=33 |issue=8 |pages=16–18 |url=https://zoosprint.zooreach.org/index.php/zp/article/view/4/4}} The eastern route is by far the more important, with 530,000 white storks using it annually, making the species the second most common migrant there (after the [[European honey buzzard]]). The flocks of migrating [[bird of prey|raptor]]s, white storks and [[great white pelican]]s can stretch for 200 km (125 mi).{{sfn|Newton|2010|pp=74–75}} The eastern route is twice as long as the western, but storks take the same time to reach the wintering grounds by either.{{sfn|Newton|2010|p=144}} Juvenile white storks set off on their first southward migration in an inherited direction, but if displaced from that bearing by weather conditions, they are unable to compensate and may end up in a new wintering location. Adults can compensate for strong winds and adjust their direction to reach their normal winter sites because they are familiar with the location. For the same reason, all spring migrants, even those from displaced wintering locations, can find their way back to the traditional breeding sites.{{sfn|Newton|2010|p=229}} An experiment with young birds raised in captivity in Kaliningrad and released in the absence of wild storks to show them the way revealed that they appeared to have an instinct to fly south, although the scatter in direction was large.{{cite journal |year= 2004 |title=Migratory orientation of first-year White Storks (''Ciconia ciconia''): Inherited information and social interactions |journal=Journal of Experimental Biology |volume= 207 |issue= 6 |pages= 937–43 |doi= 10.1242/jeb.00853 |pmid=14766952 |last1=Chernetsov |first1=Nikita |last2=Berthold |first2=Peter |last3= Querner |first3= Ulrich |doi-access= free|bibcode=2004JExpB.207..937C }} ==== Energetics ==== [[File:Whitestorkflight.png|thumb|[[Otto Lilienthal]] studied the flight of storks in 1889 while designing his glider. Sketches by his brother [[Gustav Lilienthal]].]] White storks rely on the uplift of air [[thermal]]s to soar and glide the long distances of their annual migrations between Europe and [[Sub-Saharan Africa]]. For many, the shortest route would take them over the [[Mediterranean Sea]]; however, since air thermals do not form over water, they generally detour over land to avoid the trans-Mediterranean flights that would require prolonged energetic wing flapping.{{cite journal |journal= British Birds |volume= 95 | year= 2002 |pages= 372–6 |title= Satellite-tracked Lesser Spotted Eagle avoids crossing water at the Gulf of Suez |last1= Meyburg |first1= Bernd-U. |last2= Matthes |first2= Joachim |last3= Meyburg |first3= Christiane |url= http://www.dr-meyburg.de/pdfs/a_sp100p/a_sp122.pdf |via= Raptor Research}} It has been estimated that flapping flight metabolises 23 times more body fat than soaring flight per distance travelled.{{cite journal |doi= 10.2307/3677260 |last1= Spaar |first1= Reto |last2= Bruderer |first2= Bruno |year= 1996 |title= Soaring migration of Steppe Eagles ''Aquila nipalensis'' in southern Israel: Flight behaviour under various wind and thermal conditions |journal= Journal of Avian Biology |volume= 27 |issue= 4 |pages= 289–301 |url= https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/condor/v099n02/p0458-p0469.pdf |jstor= 3677260}} Thus, flocks spiral upwards on rising warm air until they emerge at the top, up to {{Convert|1200|-|1500|m|ft|abbr = on}} above the ground (though one record from Western Sudan observed an altitude of {{Convert|3300|m|ft|abbr = on}}). Long flights over water may occasionally be undertaken. A young white stork [[Bird ringing|ringed]] at the nest in Denmark subsequently appeared in England, where it spent some days before moving on. It was later seen flying over [[St Mary's, Isles of Scilly]], and arrived in a poor condition in [[Madeira]] three days later. That island is 500 km (320 mi) from Africa, and twice as far from the European mainland.{{cite book |last1= Cocker |first1= Mark |last2= Mabey |first2= Richard |title = Birds Britannica |year= 2005 |location= London |publisher= Chatto & Windus |isbn= 978-0-7011-6907-7 |page= 58}} Migration through the [[Middle East]] may be hampered by the ''[[khamsin]]'', winds bringing gusty overcast days unsuitable for flying. In these situations, flocks of white storks sit out the adverse weather on the ground, standing and facing into the wind. == Behaviour == [[File:Ciconia ciconia -Tsavo East National Park, Kenya-8.jpg|upright|right|thumb|An adult in [[Tsavo East National Park]], Kenya. The lower parts of its legs are covered with its whitish droppings—an example of [[thermoregulation]] by [[urohidrosis]].]] The white stork is a [[wikt:gregarious|gregarious]] bird; flocks of thousands of individuals have been recorded on migration routes and at wintering areas in Africa. Non-breeding birds gather in groups of 40 or 50 during the breeding season.{{sfn|Cramp|1977|p=328}} The smaller dark-plumaged [[Abdim's stork]] is often encountered with white stork flocks in southern Africa.{{cite book |title=The Larger Illustrated Guide to Birds of Southern Africa |publisher=Struik |first1=I. |last1=Sinclair |first2= Phil |last2= Hockey | first3= N. |last3=Arlott |year=2005 |isbn=978-1-77007-243-5 |location=Cape Town, RSA |page=34}} [[Breeding pair]]s of white stork may gather in small groups to hunt, and colony nesting has been recorded in some areas.{{sfn|Cramp|1977|p=332}} However, groups among white stork colonies vary widely in size and the social structure is loosely defined; young breeding storks are often restricted to peripheral nests, while older storks attain higher breeding success while occupying the better quality nests toward the centres of breeding colonies.{{cite journal |last1=Vergara |first1=P. |last2=Aguirre |first2=J. I. |year=2006 |title=Age and breeding success related to nest position in a White Stork ''Ciconia ciconia'' colony |journal= Acta Oecologica |volume=30 |issue=3 |pages=414–418 |doi=10.1016/j.actao.2006.05.008 |bibcode=2006AcO....30..414V |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/228987920}} Social structure and group cohesion is maintained by [[altruism|altruistic]] behaviours such as [[allopreening]]. White storks exhibit this behaviour exclusively at the nest site. Standing birds preen the heads of sitting birds; sometimes these are parents grooming juveniles, and sometimes juveniles preen each other.{{cite journal |last= Harrison |first=C.J.O. |year=1965 |title=Allopreening as agonistic behaviour |journal=Behaviour |volume=24 |issue=3–4 |pages=161–209 |jstor=4533105 |doi=10.1163/156853965X00011}} Unlike most storks, it never adopts a spread-winged posture, though it is known to droop its wings (holding them away from its body with the [[primary feather]]s pointing downwards) when its plumage is wet.{{cite journal |journal=The Auk |volume=88 |issue=4 |pages=715–722 |title=Spread-wing postures and their possible functions in Ciconiidae |first= M. P. |last=Kahl |jstor=4086376 |year=1984 |url=http://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/auk/v088n04/p0715-p0722.pdf |doi=10.2307/4083833}} A white stork's droppings, containing faeces and [[uric acid]], are sometimes directed onto its own legs, making them appear white.{{cite book |title=Southern African Birds: A Photographic Guide |publisher=Struik |first1=I. |last1=Sinclair |first2=I. |last2=Davidson |year=2006 |isbn=978-1-77007-244-2 |location=Cape Town, RSA |page=34}} The resulting evaporation provides cooling and is termed [[urohidrosis]].{{cite book |title=The Sibley Guide to Bird Life and Behaviour |page=575 |publisher=Christopher Helm |location=London |isbn=978-0-7136-6250-4 |editor-last1=Elphick |editor-first1=C. |editor-last2=Dunning |editor-first2=J. B. Jr |editor-last3=Sibley |editor-first3=D. |year=2001}} Birds that have been [[bird ringing|ringed]] can sometimes be affected by the accumulation of droppings around the ring leading to constriction and leg trauma.{{cite journal|author=Schulz, H. |year=1986 |title=Thermoregulatorisches beinkoten des Weisstorchs (''Cionia ciconia''). Analyse des verhaltens und seiner bedeutung fur verluste bei beringten storchen im afrikanischen Winterquartier |journal=Vogelwarte |volume=34|pages=107–117}}{{Cite journal |last1=Calvo |first1=B. |last2=Furness |first2=R.W. |date=1992 |title=A review of the use and the effects of marks and devices on birds |journal=Ringing & Migration |volume=13 |issue=3 |pages=129–151 |doi=10.1080/03078698.1992.9674036 |doi-access=free}} The white stork has also been noted for tool use by squeezing moss in the beak to drip water into the mouths of its chicks.{{cite journal |url= http://biology.mcgill.ca/faculty/lefebvre/articles/Lefebvre_et_al_2002.pdf |title=Tools and brains in birds |last1=Lefebvre |first1=L. |last2=Nicolakakis |first2=N. |last3=Boire |first3=D. |journal=Behaviour |volume=139 |year=2002 |pages=939–973 |doi=10.1163/156853902320387918 |issue=7}} === Communication === [[File:Anschütz Vol cigogne étude 1884.png|thumb|[[Ottomar Anschütz]]'s images of white storks, taken in 1884—the earliest known photographs of any wild birds{{cite book|editor1-last=Cox|editor1-first=Rosamund Kidman|title=Wildlife Photographer of the Year: Portfolio 24 |date=2014|publisher=Firefly Books |isbn=978-0-565-09342-6 |page=13}}]] The adult white stork's main sound is noisy bill-clattering, which has been likened to distant machine gun fire. The bird makes these sounds by rapidly opening and closing its beak so that a knocking sound is made each time its beak closes. The clattering is amplified by its [[Gular skin|throat pouch]], which acts as a [[resonator]]. Used in a variety of social interactions, bill-clattering generally grows louder the longer it lasts, and takes on distinctive rhythms depending on the situation—for example, slower during copulation and briefer when given as an [[Alarm signal|alarm call]]. The only vocal sound adult birds generate is a weak, barely audible hiss; however, young birds can generate a harsh hiss, various cheeping sounds, and a cat-like mew that they use to beg for food. Like the adults, the young also clatter their beaks.{{sfn|Cramp|1977|p=334}} The ''up-down display'' is used for a number of interactions with other members of the species. Here, a stork quickly throws its head backwards so that its crown rests on its back before slowly bringing its head and neck forward again, and this is repeated several times. The display is used as a greeting between birds, post coitus, and also as a [[threat display]]. Breeding pairs are [[Territory (animal)|territorial]] over the summer, and use this display, as well as crouching forward with the tails cocked and wings extended.{{sfn|Cramp|1977|p=333}} === Breeding and lifespan === [[File:Ciconia ciconia -Iglesia de San Isidoro, Ciguenas en el campanario, Salamanca, Spain-18June2006.jpg|left|thumb|Nests on a belfry in Spain. White storks often form small nesting colonies.|alt=At least eight tall, black and white birds, in three nests on the roof of a building.]] The white stork breeds in open farmland areas with access to marshy wetlands, building a large stick [[bird nest|nest]] in trees, on buildings, or on purpose-built man-made platforms.{{cite book |editor-last1= Tryjanowski |editor-first1= Piotr |editor-last2= Sparks |editor-first2= Tim H. |editor-last3= Jerzak |editor-first3= Leszek |title= The White Stork in Poland |year= 2006 |location= Poznań, Poland |publisher= Bogucki Wydaw |isbn= 978-83-60247-35-8 }}{{page needed|date=December 2014}} Each nest is {{convert|1|–|2|m|ft|abbr=on}} in depth, {{convert|0.8|–|1.5|m|ft|abbr=on}} in diameter, and {{convert|60|–|250|kg|lb|abbr=on}} in weight. Nests are built in loose colonies. Not persecuted as it is viewed as a good omen, it often nests close to human habitation; in southern Europe, nests can be seen on churches and other buildings. The nest is typically used year after year, especially by older males. The males arrive earlier in the season and choose the nests. Larger nests are associated with greater numbers of young successfully fledged, and appear to be sought after.{{cite journal |last1=Vergara |first1=P. |last2=Gordo |first2= O. |last3=Aguirre |first3=J.I. |year= 2010 |title=Nest size, nest building behaviour and breeding success in a species with nest reuse: The White Stork ''Ciconia ciconia'' |journal=Annales Zoologici Fennici |volume=47 |pages=184–194 |doi=10.5735/086.047.0303 |issue=3 |s2cid=13804470 |url=http://www.sekj.org/PDF/anz47-free/anz47-184.pdf}} Nest change is often related to a change in the pairing and failure to raise young the previous year, and younger birds are more likely to change nesting sites.{{cite journal |title=Nest-site fidelity and breeding success in White Stork ''Ciconia ciconia'' |journal=Ibis |volume=148 |pages=672–677 |year=2006 |last1=Vergara |first1=P. |last2=Aguirre |first2= J.I. |last3=Fargallo |first3=J.A. |last4=Dávila |first4=J.A. |doi=10.1111/j.1474-919X.2006.00565.x |issue=4 |doi-access= free}} Although a pair may be found to occupy a nest, partners may change several times during the early stages and breeding activities begin only after a stable pairing is achieved.{{cite journal |last1=Wuczyński |first1=A. |title=The turnover of White Storks ''Ciconia ciconia'' on nests during spring migration |journal=Acta Ornithologica |volume=40 |issue=1 |pages=83–85 |year=2005 |doi=10.3161/068.040.0104|doi-access= free}} [[File:Аисты на гнезде (Ciconia ciconia).jpg|thumb|Mating]] Several bird species often nest within the large nests of the white stork. Regular occupants are [[house sparrow]]s, [[tree sparrow]]s, and [[common starling]]s; less common residents include [[Eurasian kestrel]]s, [[little owl]]s, [[European roller]]s, [[white wagtail]]s, [[black redstart]]s, [[Eurasian jackdaw]]s, and [[Spanish sparrow]]s.{{cite book |title=The Life of the White Stork |last=Haverschmidt |first=F. |author-link=François Haverschmidt (ornithologist) |year=1949 |publisher=E. J. Brill |location=[[Leiden]] |oclc=1576336 |pages=33–34 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=J-UUAAAAIAAJ&pg=PA33}} Active nests may attract insectivorous birds such as [[Barn swallow|swallows]], [[Western house martin|martins]], and [[Common swift|swifts]], where they prey on insects flying around.{{Cite journal |last1=Tryjanowski |first1=Piotr |last2=Jankowiak |first2=Łukasz |last3=Myczko |first3=Łukasz |last4=Mikula |first4=Peter |last5=Łuczak |first5=Andrzej |date=2024 |title=White stork ''Ciconia ciconia'' nests as an attractant to birds and bats |journal=Journal of Ornithology |volume=165 |issue=2 |pages=551–555 |doi=10.1007/s10336-023-02143-y|doi-access=free |bibcode=2024JOrni.165..551T }} Paired birds greet by engaging in up-down and head-shaking crouch displays, and clattering the beak while throwing back the head.{{sfn|Elliott|1992|pp=460-1}} Pairs copulate frequently throughout the month before eggs are laid. High-frequency pair copulation is usually associated with [[sperm competition]] and high frequency of [[extra-pair copulation]]. It has been considered that extra-pair copulation rates were low but a 2016 DNA sample study suggests that extra-pair copulation occasionally occurs in white storks.{{cite journal |title=Frequent copulations despite low sperm competition in White Storks (''Ciconia ciconia'') |last1=Tortosa |first1=F.S. |last2=Redondo |first2=T. |journal=Behaviour |volume=121 |issue=3&4 |year=1992 |pages=288–315 |jstor=4535031 |doi=10.1163/156853992X00408 |hdl=10261/60750 |hdl-access= free}}Turjeman, Sondra Feldman, et al. "Extra-pair paternity in the socially monogamous white stork (''Ciconia ciconia'') is fairly common and independent of local density." Scientific reports 6.1 (2016): 1-9.{{Cite journal |last1=Turjeman |first1=S.F. |last2=Centeno-Cuadros |first2=A. |last3=Eggers |first3=U. |last4=Rotics |first4=S. |last5=Blas |first5=J. |last6=Fiedler |first6=W. |last7=Kaatz |first7=M. |last8=Jeltsch |first8=F. |last9=Wikelski |first9=M. |last10=Nathan |first10=R. |date=2016 |title=Extra-pair paternity in the socially monogamous white stork (''Ciconia ciconia'') is fairly common and independent of local density |journal=Scientific Reports |volume=6 |issue=1 |article-number=27976 |doi=10.1038/srep27976 |pmc=4916429 |pmid=27328982|bibcode=2016NatSR...627976T}} Despite the relatively high extra-pair paternity occurrence compared to other long-lived monogamous birds, white storks form strong pair bonds and high nest fidelity maintained across years.Vergara, Pablo, et al. "Nest‐site fidelity and breeding success in White Stork ''Ciconia ciconia''." Ibis 148.4 (2006): 672-677. A white stork pair raises a single brood a year. The female typically lays four [[bird egg|eggs]], though [[clutch (eggs)|clutches]] of one to seven have been recorded.{{sfn|Cramp|1977|p=334}} The eggs are white, but often look dirty or yellowish due to a [[glutin]]ous covering. They typically measure {{cvt|73|x|52|mm}}, and weigh {{cvt|96|–|129|g}},{{sfn|Cramp|1977|p=334}} of which about {{cvt|11|g}} is shell.{{cite book |title= Oologia Neerlandica: Eggs of Birds Breeding in the Netherlands |volume=II |last=van Pelt Lechner |first= A. A. |year=1911 |publisher=Nijhof |location=The Hague, Netherlands |page=118 |url=https://archive.org/details/oologianeerlandi02pelt/page/n148}} Incubation begins as soon as the first egg is laid, so the brood hatches asynchronously, beginning 33 to 34 days later. The first hatchling typically has a competitive edge over the others. While stronger chicks are not aggressive towards weaker siblings, as is the case in some species, weak or small chicks are sometimes killed by their parents.{{cite journal |last1=Zielinski |first1=P. |year=2002 |title=Brood reduction and parental infanticide– are the White Stork ''Ciconia ciconia'' and the Black Stork ''C. nigra'' exceptional? |journal=Acta Ornithologica |volume=37 |issue=2 |pages=113–119 |doi=10.3161/068.037.0207 |s2cid= 62888943 |doi-access= free}}{{cite journal |doi=10.2307/3676447 |title= Motives for parental infanticide in White Storks ''Ciconia ciconia'' |first1=F.S. |last1= Tortosa |last2= Redondo |first2=T. |journal=Ornis Scandinavica |volume=23 |issue=2 |year=1992 |pages=185–189 |jstor=3676447}} This behavior occurs in times of food shortage to reduce brood size and hence increase the chance of survival of the remaining nestlings. White stork nestlings do not attack each other, and their parents' feeding method (disgorging large amounts of food at once) means that stronger siblings cannot directly outcompete weaker ones for food; hence, parental infanticide is an efficient way to reduce brood size. Despite this, the behavior has not been commonly observed. [[File:Cigogne blanche MHNT.jpg|upright|left|thumb|Egg]] The temperature and weather around the time of hatching in spring are important; cool temperatures and wet weather increase chick mortality and reduce breeding success rates. Somewhat unexpectedly, studies have found that later-hatching chicks which successfully reach adulthood produce more chicks than do their earlier-hatching nestmates.{{cite journal |journal= Evolutionary Ecology Research |year=2007 |volume=9 |pages=355–364 |title=Younger, weaker White Stork (''Ciconia ciconia'') nestlings become the best breeders |last1=Aguirre |first1= J.I. |last2=Vergara |first2=P. |url=http://www.ucm.es/data/cont/media/www/pag-33468/2007_EvolEcolRes_9_355.pdf}} The body weight of the chicks increases rapidly in the first few weeks and reaches a plateau of about {{cvt|3.4|kg}} in 45 days. The length of the beak increases linearly for about 50 days.{{cite journal |last1=Tsachalidis |first1=E.P. |last2=Liordos |first2=V. |last3=Goutner |first3=V. |year=2005| title=Growth of White Stork ''Ciconia ciconia'' nestlings |journal=Ardea |volume=93 | issue=1 |pages=133–137 |url=http://users.auth.gr/~vgoutner/pdf/50.pdf |via= [[Aristotle University of Thessaloniki]]}} Young birds are fed with earthworms and insects, which are regurgitated by the parents onto the floor of the nest. Older chicks reach into the mouths of parents to obtain food.{{cite journal |last1=Mužinic |first1=J. |last2=Rašajski |first2=J. |year=1992 |title=On food and feeding habits in the White Stork, ''Ciconia c. ciconia'', in the central Balkans |journal=Ökologie der Vögel (Ecology of Birds) |volume=14 |pages=211–223}} Chicks fledge 58 to 64 days after hatching.{{sfn|Van den Bossche|2002|p=8}} White storks generally begin breeding when about four years old, although the age of first breeding has been recorded as early as two years and as late as seven years.{{sfn|Cramp|1977|p=335}} The oldest known wild white stork lived for 39 years after being [[bird ringing|ringed]] in Switzerland,{{cite web |url=http://www.euring.org/data_and_codes/longevity-voous.htm |title=EURING list of longevity records for European birds |publisher=EURING |year= 2010 |access-date= 2 December 2010}} while captive birds have lived for more than 35 years.{{sfn|Elliott|1992|pp=460-1}} === Feeding === White storks consume a wide variety of animal prey. They prefer to forage in meadows that are within roughly 5 km (3 mi) of their nest and sites where the vegetation is shorter so that their prey is more accessible. Their diet varies according to season, locality and prey availability. Common food items include insects (primarily beetles, grasshoppers, locusts and crickets), earthworms, reptiles, amphibians, particularly frog species such as the [[edible frog]] (''Pelophylax'' kl. ''esculentus'') and [[common frog]] (''Rana temporaria'') and small mammals such as voles, moles and shrews. Less commonly, they also eat bird eggs and young birds, fish, molluscs, crustaceans and scorpions. They hunt mainly during the day, swallowing small prey whole, but killing and breaking apart larger prey before swallowing.{{sfn|Cramp|1977|p=332}} Rubber bands are mistaken for earthworms and consumed, occasionally resulting in fatal blockage of the digestive tract.{{Cite journal |last1=Henry |first1=P.Y. |last2=Wey |first2=G.R. |last3=Balança |first3=G. |doi=10.1675/063.034.0414 |title=Rubber band ingestion by a rubbish dump dweller, the White Stork (''Ciconia ciconia'') |journal=Waterbirds |volume=34 |issue=4 |pages=504–508 |year=2011 |s2cid= 84701745}} [[File:Stork picking at rabbit.jpg|thumbnail|White stork picking at a dead young [[European rabbit]] (''Oryctolagus cuniculus'')]] Birds returning to Latvia during spring have been shown to locate their prey, moor frogs (''[[Rana arvalis]]''), by homing in on the mating calls produced by aggregations of male frogs.{{cite journal |title=White Storks ''Ciconia ciconia'' eavesdrop on mating calls of Moor Frogs ''Rana arvalis'' |last1=Igaune |first1=K. |first2=I. |last2=Krams |last3=Krama |first3=T. |last4=Bobkova |first4=J. |journal=Journal of Avian Biology |year=2008 |volume=39 |issue=2 |pages=229–232 |doi=10.1111/j.2008.0908-8857.04180.x |url=http://ecolevol.biology.lv/faili/upload/2008_JAB%20Igaune%20Krama%20Krams.pdf |access-date=2010-12-10 |archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20110722161119/http://ecolevol.biology.lv/faili/upload/2008_JAB%20Igaune%20Krama%20Krams.pdf |archive-date= 2011-07-22 }} The diet of non-breeding birds is similar to that of breeding birds, but food items are more often taken from dry areas.{{cite journal |last1=Antczak |first1=M. |last2=Konwerski |first2=S. |last3=Grobelny |first3=S. |last4=Tryjanowski |first4= P. |doi=10.1675/1524-4695(2002)025[0424:TFCOIA]2.0.CO;2|s2cid= 86314392}} White storks wintering in western India have been observed to follow [[blackbuck]] to capture insects disturbed by them.{{cite journal |last1=Parasharya |first1=B.M. |last2=Vyas |first2=R. |year=1998| title=Foraging association of White Stork ''Ciconia ciconia'' with Blackbuck ''Antilope cervicapra''| journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume=95|issue=1 |page=112 |url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48604632}} Wintering white storks in India sometimes forage along with the [[Asian woolly-necked stork]] (''Ciconia episcopus'').{{cite journal |last1=Pande |first1=S. |last2=Sant |first2=N. |last3= Bhate |first3=R. |last4= Ponkshe |first4=A. |last5=Pandit |first5=P. |last6=Pawashe |first6=A. Akhtar |first1= S.A. |last2=Tiwari |first2=J.K. |year=1993 |title=Food piracy by a White Stork ''Ciconia ciconia'' (Linn.) |journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume=90 |issue=1 the breeding season, migration period and winter.{{cite journal |author1=Arizaga, J. |author2=Resano-Mayor, J. |author3=Villanúa, D. |author4=Alonso, D. |author5=Barbarin, J.M. |author6=Herrero, A. |author7=Lekuona, J.M. |author8=Rodriguez, R. |year=2018| title=Importance of artificial stopover sites through avian migration flyways: a landfill-based assessment with the White Stork ''Ciconia ciconia'' |journal=Ibis|volume=160| pages=542–553 |doi=10.1111/ibi.12566| issue=3}} == Parasites and diseases == White stork nests are [[habitat]]s for an array of small arthropods, particularly over the warmer months after the birds arrive to breed. Nesting over successive years, the storks bring more material to line their nests, and layers of organic material accumulate within them. Not only do their bodies tend to regulate temperatures within the nest, but excrement, food remains and feather and skin fragments provide nourishment for a large and diverse population of free-living [[mesostigmata|mesostigmatic]] mites. A survey of twelve nests found 13,352 individuals of 34 species, the most common being ''[[Macrocheles merdarius]]'', ''[[Macrocheles robustulus|M. robustulus]]'', ''Uroobovella pyriformis'' and ''Trichouropoda orbicularis'', which together represented almost 85% of all the specimens collected. These feed on the eggs and larvae of insects and on [[nematode]]s, which are abundant in the nest litter. These mites are dispersed by [[coprophagia|coprophilous]] beetles, often of the family [[Scarabaeidae]], or on dung brought by the storks during nest construction. Parasitic mites do not occur, perhaps being controlled by the predatory species. The overall effect of the mite population is unclear; the mites may play a role in suppressing harmful organisms (and thus be beneficial), or may themselves have an adverse effect on nestlings.{{cite journal |last1= Błoszyk |first1= Jerzy |last2= Gwiazdowicz |first2= Dariusz J. |last3= Bajerlein |first3= Daria |last4= Halliday |first4= Robert Bruce | year=2005 | title= Nests of the White Stork ''Ciconia ciconia'' (L.) as a habitat for Mesostigmatic Mites (Acari, Mesostigmata) | journal= Acta Parasitologica | volume= 50 | issue = 2 | pages= 171–75 | url = http://www.actaparasitologica.pan.pl/archive/PDF/Bloszyk.pdf }}{{cite journal |last1= Bajerlein |first1= Daria |last2= Błoszyk |first2= Jerzy |last3= Gwiazdowicz |first3= Dariusz J. |last4= Ptaszyk |first4= Jerzy |last5= Halliday |first5= Bruce |display-authors= 4 |year= 2006 |title= Community structure and dispersal of mites (Acari, Mesostigmata) in nests of the White Stork (''Ciconia ciconia'') |journal= Biologia |volume= 61 |issue= 5 |pages= 525–30 |doi= 10.2478/s11756-006-0086-9 |doi-access= free |bibcode= 2006Biolg..61..525B }} The birds themselves host species belonging to more than four genera of [[feather mite]]s.{{cite book |title= Fleas, Flukes and Cuckoos.
