stork also carries several types of internal parasites, including ''[[Toxoplasma gondii]]''{{cite book |title= Foodborne Parasites |first= Ynes R. |last= Ortega |year= 2006 |location=New York |publisher= Springer |page= 121 |isbn= 978-0-387-30068-9}} and [[intestinal parasite]]s of the genus ''[[Giardia]]''.{{cite journal |title=Giardiasis in a White Stork in The Netherlands |first1= F.F. |last1= Franssen |first2= J. |last2= Hooimeijer |first3= B. |last3= Blankenstein |first4= Dirk J. |last4= Houwers |journal= Journal of Wildlife Diseases |volume= 36 |issue= 4 |pages= 764–6 |year= 2000 |pmid= 11085441 |doi= 10.7589/0090-3558-36.4.764|s2cid= 45894419 |doi-access= free }} A study of 120 white stork carcasses from [[Saxony-Anhalt]] and [[Brandenburg]] in Germany yielded eight species of [[trematoda|trematode]] (fluke), four [[cestoda|cestode]] (tapeworm) species, and at least three species of [[nematoda|nematode]].{{cite journal |last1=Schuster |first1= Rolf |last2= Schaffer |first2= Thoralf |last3= Shimalov |first3= Vladimir |year= 2002 |title= [The helminth fauna of indigenous White Storks (''Ciconia ciconia'')] |journal= Berliner und Munchener Tierarztliche Wochenschrift |volume= 115 |pages= 435–9 |pmid= 12481650 |language=de |issue= 11–12}} One species of fluke, ''Chaunocephalus ferox'', caused lesions in the wall of the small intestine in a number of birds admitted to two rehabilitation centres in central Spain, and was associated with reduced weight. It is a recognised pathogen and cause of morbidity in the [[Asian openbill]] (''Anastomus oscitans'').{{cite journal |last1= Höfle |year= 2003 |title= ''Chaunocephalus ferox'' in free-living White Storks in central Spain |journal= Avian Diseases |volume= 47 |issue= 2 |pages= 506–12 |doi= 10.1637/0005-2086(2003)047[0506:CFIFWS]2.0.CO;2 |first1= U. |last2= Krone |first2=O. |last3= Blanco |first3= J.M. |last4= Pizarro |first4= M. |pmid= 12887215|s2cid= 32009094 }} More recently, the thorough study performed by J. Sitko and P. Heneberg in the Czech Republic in 1962–2013 suggested that the central European white storks host 11 helminth species. ''Chaunocephalus ferox'', ''Tylodelphys excavata'' and ''Dictymetra discoidea'' were reported to be the dominant ones. The other species found included ''Cathaemasia hians'', ''Echinochasmus spinulosus'', ''[[Echinostoma revolutum]]'', ''Echinostoma sudanense'', ''Duboisia syriaca'', ''Apharyngostrigea cornu'', ''[[Capillaria (nematode)|Capillaria]]'' sp. and ''Dictymetra discoidea''. Juvenile white storks were shown to host less species, but the intensity of infection was higher in the juveniles than in the adult storks.{{Cite journal |last1= Sitko |first1=J. |last2= Heneberg |first2= P. |year= 2015 |title= Composition, structure and pattern of helminth assemblages associated with central European storks (Ciconiidae) |journal= Parasitology International |volume= 64 |issue=2 |pages= 130–4 |doi= 10.1016/j.parint.2014.11.004|pmid=25462710 }} [[West Nile virus]] (WNV) is mainly a bird infection that is transmitted between birds by [[mosquito]]s.{{cite journal |last1= Hayes |first1= Edward B. |last2= Komar |first2= Nicholas |last3= Nasci |first3= Roger S. |last4= Montgomery |first4= Susan P. |last5= O'Leary |first5= Daniel R. |last6= Campbell |first6= Grant L. |display-authors= 4 |title= Epidemiology and transmission dynamics of West Nile Virus Disease |journal= Emerging Infectious Diseases |volume= 11 |issue= 8 |pages= 1167–73 |year= 2005 |pmid= 16102302 |doi= 10.3201/eid1108.050289a |pmc= 3320478}} Migrating birds appear to be important in spread of the virus,{{cite book |last1= Malkinson |year= 2002 |volume= 267 |pages= 309–22 |pmid= 12082995 |first1=M. |last2= Banet |first2= C. |title= Japanese Encephalitis and West Nile Viruses |chapter= The Role of Birds in the Ecology of West Nile Virus in Europe and Africa |series= Current Topics in Microbiology and Immunology |doi= 10.1007/978-3-642-59403-8_15 |isbn= 978-3-642-63966-1}} the ecology of which remains poorly known.{{cite journal |last1=Zeller |first1= Hervé G. |last2= Schuffenecker |first2= Isabelle |year= 2004 |title= West Nile Virus: An overview of its spread in Europe and the Mediterranean Basin in contrast to its spread in the Americas |journal= [[European Journal of Clinical Microbiology & Infectious Diseases]] |volume= 23 |issue= 3 |pages= 147–56 |doi= 10.1007/s10096-003-1085-1 |pmid= 14986160|s2cid= 24372103 }} On 26 August 1998, a flock of about 1,200 migrating white storks that had been blown off course on their southward journey landed in [[Eilat]], in southern Israel. The flock was stressed as it had resorted to flapping flight to return to its migratory route, and a number of birds died. A virulent strain of West Nile virus was isolated from the brains of eleven dead juveniles. Other white storks subsequently tested in Israel have shown anti-WNV [[antibody|antibodies]].{{cite journal |title= Introduction of West Nile virus in the Middle East by migrating White Storks |last1= Malkinson |first1= Mertyn |last2= Banet |first2= Caroline |last3= Weisman |first3= Yoram |last4= Pokamunski |first4= Shimon |last5= King |first5= Roni |last6= Drouet |first6= Marie-Thérèse |last7= Deubel |first7= Vincent |display-authors= 4 |journal= Emerging Infectious Diseases |year= 2002 |volume= 8 |issue= 4 |pages= 392–7 |pmc= 2730252 |pmid= 11971773 |doi= 10.3201/eid0804.010217}} In 2008 three juvenile white storks from a Polish wildlife refuge yielded [[Serostatus|seropositive]] results indicating exposure to the virus, but the context or existence of the virus in Poland is unclear.{{cite journal |journal= Viral Immunology |volume= 21 |issue= 2 |pages=247–54 |last1= Hubálek |first1= Zdenek |last2= Wegner |first2= Elżbieta |last3= Halouzka |first3= Jiří |last4= Tryjanowski |first4= Piotr |last5= Jerzak |first5= Leszek |last6= Sikutová |first6= Silvie |last7= Rudolf |first7= Ivo |last8= Kruszewicz |first8= Andrzej G. |last9= Jaworski |first9= Zbigniew |last10= Wlodarczyk |first10= Radoslaw |display-authors= 4 |title= Serologic survey of potential vertebrate hosts for West Nile Virus in Poland |year= 2008 |doi= 10.1089/vim.2007.0111|pmid= 18433332 }} == Conservation == [[File:Stork Census 2014-2015.svg|thumb|right|upright=1.6| {{Center|White stork census by country (2014–2015)}} by breeding pairs {| |- | {{Color box|#ffffcc}} || 0–950 |- | {{Color box|#dcf1af}} || 951–2,931 |- | {{Color box|#b7e194}} || 2,932–6,153 |- | {{Color box|#8dcf82}} || 6,154–9,000 |- | {{Color box|#64bc6e}} || 9,001–14,300 |- | {{Color box|#3ba859}} || 14,301–23,000 |- | {{Color box|#1c8a48}} || 23,001–46,000 |- | {{Color box|#006837}} || 46,001–52,700 |- | {{Color box|#E0E0E0}} || No data |} ]] The white stork's decline due to [[industrialisation]] and agricultural changes (principally the draining of wetlands and conversion of meadows to crops such as [[maize]]) began in the 19th century: the last wild individual in Belgium was seen in 1895, in Sweden in 1955, in Switzerland in 1950 and in the Netherlands in 1991. However, the species has since been reintroduced to many regions.{{cite journal |last1=Berthold |first1= Peter |last2= Fiedler |first2= Wolfgang |last3= Querner |first3= Ulrich |title=White Stork (''Ciconia ciconia'') migration studies: Basic research devoted to conservation measures |pages= 133–41 |volume= 2 |year= 2000 |journal= Global Environment Research}} It has been rated as [[least concern]] by the IUCN since 1994, after being evaluated as [[near threatened]] in 1988. The white stork is one of the species to which the ''Agreement on the Conservation of African-Eurasian Migratory Waterbirds'' ([[AEWA]]) applies.{{cite web|title=Annex 2: Waterbird Species to Which the Agreement Applies |work=Agreement on the Conservation of African-Eurasian Migratory Waterbirds (AEWA) |url=http://www.unep-aewa.org/documents/agreement_text/eng/pdf/aewa_agreement_text_annex2.pdf |publisher=UNEP/ AEWA Secretariat |access-date=9 December 2010 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110728144028/http://www.unep-aewa.org/documents/agreement_text/eng/pdf/aewa_agreement_text_annex2.pdf |archive-date=28 July 2011 }} Parties to the agreement are required to engage in a wide range of conservation strategies described in a detailed action plan. The plan is intended to address key issues such as species and habitat conservation, management of human activities, research, education, and implementation.{{cite web|title=Introduction |work=African-Eurasian Waterbird Agreement |url=http://www.unep-aewa.org/about/introduction.htm |publisher=UNEP/ AEWA Secretariat |access-date=9 December 2010 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120211082252/http://www.unep-aewa.org/about/introduction.htm |archive-date=11 February 2012 }} Threats include the continued loss of wetlands, collisions with overhead power lines, use of [[Persistent organic pollutant|persistent pesticides]] (such as [[DDT]]) to combat [[desert locust|locust]]s in Africa, and largely illegal hunting on migration routes and wintering grounds.{{sfn|Elliott|1992|pp=460-1}} [[File:Ciconia ciconia juv small.jpg|thumb|left|alt=Three long-legged, long-billed black and white birds stand on a huge pile of sticks atop an artificial platform on a pole|A nest on a man-made platform in Poland. The platform was built as a conservation measure and to prevent storks from disrupting electricity supplies through nesting on pylons. Three young white storks are on the nest, and two [[Eurasian tree sparrow]]s are perching on the side of the nest.]] A large population of white storks breeds in central (Poland, Ukraine and Germany) and southern Europe (Spain and Turkey). In a 2004/05 census, there were 52,500 pairs in [[Poland]], 30,000 pairs in [[Ukraine]], 20,000 pairs in [[Belarus]], 13,000 pairs in [[Lithuania]] (the highest known density of this species in the world), 10,700 pairs in [[Latvia]], and 10,200 in [[Russia]]. There were around 5,500 pairs in [[Romania]], 5,300 in [[Hungary]], and an estimated 4,956 breeding pairs in [[Bulgaria]].{{cite web |title= Preliminary Results of the VI International White Stork Census 2004/05 |url= http://bergenhusen.nabu.de/zensus/zensus2006/worldpopulation.pdf |publisher= Naturschutzbund Deutschland (NABU) |archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20110812085950/http://bergenhusen.nabu.de/zensus/zensus2006/worldpopulation.pdf |archive-date= 12 August 2011}} In former [[Yugoslavia]] there are 1,700 in [[Croatia]], 1,400 in [[Serbia]],{{cite web |title=Bele rode - Društvo za zaštitu i proučavanje ptica Srbije|url=https://pticesrbije.rs/kamera-rode/ |work= pticesrbije.rs |access-date=26 December 2023|language=Serbian}}{{cite web |title=Rekordan broj belih roda na "Rodiću": U 52 gnezda parovi sa svojim mladuncima|url=https://www.021.rs/story/Novi-Sad/Vesti/346073/FOTO-Rekordan-broj-belih-roda-na-Rodicu-U-52-gnezda-parovi-sa-svojim-mladuncima.html |work= 021.rs|date=7 July 2023|access-date=26 December 2023|language=Serbian}} 236 in [[Slovenia]] and an estimated 40 breeding pairs in [[Bosnia and Herzegovina]]. In [[Germany]], the majority of the total 4,482 pairs were in the eastern region, especially in the states of [[Brandenburg]] and [[Mecklenburg-Vorpommern]] (1296 and 863 pairs in 2008 respectively).{{cite web |title= National data |url= http://bergenhusen.nabu.de/weissstorch/national/|publisher= Naturschutzbund Deutschland (NABU) |access-date= 14 January 2012}} Apart from [[Spain]] and [[Portugal]] (33,217 and 7,684 pairs in 2004/05 respectively), populations are generally much less stable. In the eastern Mediterranean region, [[Turkey]] has a sizeable population of 6,195 pairs, and [[Greece]] 2,139 pairs. In Western Europe, the white stork remains a rare bird despite conservation efforts. In 2004, [[France]] had only 973 pairs, and the [[Netherlands]] had 528 pairs. In Denmark, the species had consistently bred since the 15th century, peaking at several thousand pairs around 1800. Afterwards, it began declining mainly due to habitat loss (especially conversion of wetlands and meadows into modern farming), with only a few tens of breeding pairs in 1974 and none in 2008.{{cite web| title=Svag fremgang for storken: Udsigt til otte par storke i Danmark |url=https://www.dof.dk/om-dof/nyheder?nyhed_id=2069 | date=30 May 2022 | publisher=[[Dansk Ornitologisk Forening]] | access-date=28 June 2022 }}{{cite web| title=Hvid stork | url=https://www.naturhistoriskmuseum.dk/viden/naturlex/fugle/hvid-stork | publisher=[[Natural History Museum, Aarhus]] | access-date=16 June 2022 }} Since then, it has reestablished itself and the population has slowly started to increase, reaching ten pairs in 2023.{{cite news| title=Flere danske storkeunger i 2023 | url=https://naturguide.dk/fremgang-for-danske-ynglestorke/ | date=9 July 2023 | publisher=Naturguide | access-date=14 November 2023 }} In Armenia, the population of the white stork slightly increased in the period between 2005 and 2015, and by the last data reached 652 pairs.Aghababyan K. 2011. White Storks (''Ciconia ciconia'' L.): Population tendencies in Armenia. // Proceedings of the International Conference "Biological Diversity and Conservation Problems of the Fauna of the Caucasus". September 26–29, Yerevan, Asoghik: 9-13.Aghababyan K., Kochinyan M., and Stepanyan L. (2013). White Storks (''Ciconia ciconia'' L.) in Armenia: population, trend, and relationships to humans. // in Hötker, H. & K.-M. Thomsen (ed): White Stork on the top? - Results of the VIth International White Stork Census 2004/05. NABU (Naturschutzbund Deutschland e.V.), Berlin.White Stork in Armenia. 2016. Armenian Bird Census Council. Retrieved from http://www.abcc-am.org/white-stork.html [[File:Haikara (White stork) (1) Koski Tl. 9.7.2015.jpg|thumb|The first known pair in Finland (2015), representing a northward expansion compared to the species' historical breeding range]] In the early 1980s, the population had fallen to fewer than nine pairs in the entire upper [[Rhine River]] valley, an area closely identified with the white stork for centuries. Conservation efforts successfully increased the population of birds there to 270 pairs (in 2008), largely due to the actions of the Association for the Protection and Reintroduction of Storks in [[Alsace]] and [[Lorraine (region)|Lorraine]].{{cite news|title=French region saves iconic White Storks from brink: population hits 270 pairs after dwindling to nine |first=Molly |last= Moore |page= A10 |date= 22 June 2008 |newspaper= [[The Washington Post]] |url= https://www.washingtonpost.com/wp-dyn/content/article/2008/06/21/AR2008062101774.html}} The reintroduction of [[zoo]]-reared birds has halted further declines in Italy, the Netherlands, and Switzerland. There were 601 pairs breeding in [[Armenia]] and around 700 pairs in the Netherlands in 2008,{{cite web |url= http://www.birdlife.org/eu/pdfs/Case_study_Restoring_Dutch_White_Stork_population.pdf |title= Restoring the Dutch White Stork Population: A Charismatic Species Brought Back by Targeted Conservation Work |last= Altenburg |first= Jouke |year= 2010 |work= BirdLife International |publisher= Vogelbescherming Netherlands (BirdLife Netherlands) |access-date= 30 January 2011}} and few pairs also breed in South Africa, typically recent colonists from within the normal wintering population.{{sfn|Elliott|1992|pp=460-1}} In Poland, electric poles have been modified with a platform at the top to prevent the white stork's large nest from disrupting the electricity supply, and sometimes nests are moved from an electric pole to a man-made platform.{{cite journal |last1= Tryjanowski |first1= Piotr |last2=Kosicki |first2= Jakub Z. |last3= Kuzniak |first3= Stanisław |last4= Sparks |first4= Tim H. |year= 2009 |title= Long-term changes and breeding success in relation to nesting structures used by the White Stork, ''Ciconia ciconia'' |journal= Annales Zoologici Fennici |volume= 46 |pages= 34–8 |url= http://www.sekj.org/PDF/anz46-free/anz46-034.pdf |doi= 10.5735/086.046.0104|s2cid= 13777240 }} Introductions of zoo-reared birds in the Netherlands has been followed up by feeding and nest-building programs by volunteers. Similar reintroduction programs are taking place in Sweden,{{cite web |title= The Swedish White Stork Reintroduction Program |url= http://www.skof.se/stork/sida_historik_english.shtml |work= Valkommen till Storkprojektet! |date= 17 December 2010 |publisher= Scanian Ornithological Society (SkOF) |access-date= 8 February 2011 |archive-url= https://archive.today/20121225100736/http://www.skof.se/stork/sida_historik_english.shtml |archive-date= 25 December 2012 }} and Switzerland,{{cite journal |url= http://www.avibirds.com/pdf/O/Ooievaar9.pdf |title= Time budget, habitat use and breeding success of White Storks (''Ciconia ciconia'') under variable foraging conditions during the breeding season in Switzerland |last1= Moritzi |first1= Martin |last2= Maumary |first2= Lionel |last3= Schmid |first3= David |last4= Steiner |first4= Isabelle |last5= Vallotton |first5= Laurent |last6= Spaar |first6= Reto |last7= Biber |first7= Olivier |display-authors= 4 |year= 2001 |journal= Ardea |volume= 89 |issue= 3 |pages= 457–70}} where 175 pairs were recorded breeding in 2000.{{cite journal |url= http://www.cefe.cnrs.fr/biom/pdf/Schaub-BioCon-2004.pdf |title= Is the reintroduced White Stork (''Ciconia ciconia'') population in Switzerland self-sustainable? |last1= Schaub |first1= Michael |last2= Pradela |first2= Roger |last3= Lebretona |first3= Jean-Dominique |doi= 10.1016/j.biocon.2003.11.002 |journal= Biological Conservation |volume= 119 |issue= 1 |year= 2004 |pages= 105–14 |bibcode= 2004BCons.119..105S |access-date= 2011-02-08 |archive-date= 2011-07-18 |archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20110718164931/http://www.cefe.cnrs.fr/biom/pdf/Schaub-BioCon-2004.pdf }} Long-term viability of the population in Switzerland is unclear as breeding success rates are low, and supplementary feeding does not appear to be of benefit. However, as of 2017, 470 adults and 757 young ones were recorded in Switzerland.{{Cite web|url=https://www.swissinfo.ch/eng/sci-tech/reluctant-migrants_switzerland-sees-record-stork-numbers-but-fewer-leave/44060124|title=Switzerland sees record stork numbers|website=SWI swissinfo.ch|date=19 April 2018 }} Historically, the species' northern breeding limit was at [[Estonia]], but it has moved slowly northwards (possibly due to [[Global warming|warmer temperatures]]) into [[Karelia]] and in 2015 the first ever known breeding happened in [[Finland]]. In August 2019, 24 juveniles were released at the Knepp Estate in [[West Sussex]], and others at a site near [[Tunbridge Wells]] and at the Wintershall Estate, near [[Godalming]], as part of a project to reintroduce the white stork as a breeding species in [[South East England]],{{cite web |title=White Storks released in Surrey and Sussex |url=https://www.birdguides.com/news/white-storks-released-in-surrey-and-sussex/ |website=[[BirdGuides]] |access-date=19 August 2019 |language=en |date=19 August 2019}} for the first time since 1416.{{cite news |last1=Crisp |first1=Wil |title=White storks to breed in Britain for the first time in 600 years |url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/news/2018/07/07/white-storks-breed-britain-first-time-600-years/ |archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/archive/20220112/https://www.telegraph.co.uk/news/2018/07/07/white-storks-breed-britain-first-time-600-years/ |archive-date=2022-01-12 |url-access=subscription |url-status=live |website=The Telegraph |date=7 July 2018}}{{cbignore}} In 2020, the program was successful with the birth of five baby storks. == Cultural associations == [[File:Józef Chełmoński - Bociany - 01 (MP 561 MNW).jpg|thumb|300px|''Bociany'' (''Storks''), an [[oil painting]] of 1900, 150×198 cm, by [[Józef Chełmoński]] (1849–1914), [[National Museum in Warsaw]]]] [[File:Stork moral education.jpg|thumb|upright|Supposed filial virtues of the stork in a children's moral education text from 1831{{cite book |author= Unknown |title= Descriptive Scenes for Children |publisher= N.S. and S.G. Simpkins |location= Boston |year= 1831 |page= 3 |oclc= 31373438 |url= https://archive.org/stream/descriptivescene00bostiala#page/n1/mode/1up |via= the [[Internet Archive]]}}]] Due to its large size, predation on vermin, and nesting behaviour close to human settlements and on rooftops, the white stork has an imposing presence that has influenced human culture and folklore. [[Greek mythology|Greek]] and [[Roman mythology]] portray storks as models of parental devotion. The Trojan princess [[Antigone (daughter of Laomedon)|Antigone]] was transformed into a white stork by the goddess [[Hera]] for trying to compete with the goddess in beauty.{{cite book|last=Pepin|first=Ronald|title=The Vatican Mythographers|location=New York|publisher=Fordham University Press|date=2008|isbn=978-0-8232-2892-8|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=sE7WnkLLt2gC|pages=77, 135}} The 3rd century Roman writer [[Claudius Aelianus|Aelian]] citing the authority of Alexander of Myndus noted in his ''De natura animalium'' (book 3, chapter 23) that aged storks flew away to oceanic islands where they were transformed into humans as a reward for their piety towards their parents.{{cite book|url=https://archive.org/stream/L446AelianCharacteristicsOfAnimalsI15/L446-Aelian%20Characteristics%20of%20Animals%20I%3A1-5#page/n104/mode/1up/|title=Aelian on the characteristics of animals. Volume I. Books I-V.|publisher=Harvard University Press|year=1958|editor=Schofield, A.F.|page=185}} The bird is featured in at least three of [[Aesop's Fables]]: [[The Fox and the Stork]], [[The Farmer and the Stork]], and [[The Frogs Who Desired a King]]. Storks were also thought to care for their aged parents, feeding them and even transporting them, and children's books depicted them as a model of filial values. A Greek law called ''Pelargonia'', from the [[Ancient Greek]] word ''pelargos'' for stork, required citizens to take care of their aged parents.{{sfn|Elliott|1992|pp=460-1}} The Greeks also held that killing a stork could be punished with death.{{cite book |chapter= The White Stork ''Ciconia ciconia'' Protection in Poland by Fradition, Customs, Law, and Active Efforts |last= Dolata |first= Pawel T. |editor-last1= Tryjanowski |editor1-first= Piotr |editor-last2= Sparks |editor-first2= Tim H. |editor-last3= Jerzak |editor-first3= L. |title= The White Stork in Poland: Studies in Biology, Ecology and Conservation |publisher= Bogucki Wydawnictwo Naukowe |location= Poznań, Poland |year= 2006 |isbn= 978-83-60247-35-8}} Storks were allegedly protected in [[Ancient Thessaly]] as they hunted snakes, and widely held to be [[Virgil]]'s "white bird".{{cite book |last= Royds |first= Thomas Fletcher |title= The Beasts, Birds, and Bees of Virgil; A Naturalist's Handbook to the Georgics |publisher= [[Oxford University Press]] |year= 1914 |url= https://archive.org/stream/beastsbirdsbeeso00roydrich#page/36/mode/2up/search/Stork |via= The [[Internet Archive]]}} Roman writers noted the white stork's arrival in spring, which alerted farmers to plant their vines.{{cite journal |last1= Thomas |first1= Richard F. |year= 1988 |title= Vergil's "White Bird" and the Alexandrian reference (G. 2. 319-20) |journal= Classical Philology |volume= 83 |issue= 3 |pages= 214–7 |url= http://dash.harvard.edu/bitstream/handle/1/3775754/Thomas_Vergil_WhiteBird.pdf?sequence=2 |doi= 10.1086/367107|s2cid= 161540189 }} On occasion ancient Egyptians mummified White storks.{{cite journal |last1=Gautier |first1=Achilles |date=2005 |title=Animal Mummies and Remains from the Necropolis of Elkab (Upper Egypt) |url=https://revistas.uam.es/archaeofauna/article/view/7441 |journal=archaeofauna |volume=14 |pages=139–170 |access-date=25 December 2023}} Followers of [[Islam]] revered storks because they made an annual pilgrimage to [[Mecca]] on their migration. Some of the earliest understandings of bird migration were initiated by an interest in white storks; ''[[Pfeilstorch|Pfeilstörche]]'' ("arrow storks") were found in Europe with African [[arrow]]s embedded in their bodies. A well-known example of such a stork found in the summer of 1822 in the German town of [[Klütz]] in [[Mecklenburg]] was made into a mounted taxidermy specimen, complete with the ornate African arrow, that is now in the [[University of Rostock]].{{cite journal |last= Kinzelbach |first= Ragnar |year= 2003 |title= Der Pfeilstorch in der Zoologischen Sammlung der Universität Rostock |journal= Der Sprössling: Fachschaftsrat Biologie der Universität Rostock |issue= 3 |language= de |pages= 9–10 |url= http://www.biofachschaft.uni-rostock.de/fileadmin/MathNat_Bio_Fachschaft/Sproessling_Nr._3_SS03.pdf |archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20141125180016/http://www.biofachschaft.uni-rostock.de/fileadmin/MathNat_Bio_Fachschaft/Sproessling_Nr._3_SS03.pdf |archive-date= 2014-11-25 }} Storks have little fear of humans, provided they are not disturbed, and often nest on buildings in Europe. In Germany, the presence of a nest on a house was believed to protect against fires. They were also protected because of the belief that their souls were human.{{cite book |author-link= Charles Hardwick (antiquary) |last= Hardwick |first= Charles |title= Traditions, Superstition and Folk-lore |publisher= A. Ireland and Co |location= Manchester, UK |year= 1872 |url= https://archive.org/stream/traditionssuper00unkngoog#page/n270/mode/1up/ |pages= 238, 247–8 |via= The [[Internet Archive]]}} German, Dutch and Polish households would encourage storks to nest on houses, sometimes by constructing purpose-built high platforms, to bring good luck.{{cite journal |title= The stork fable−some psychodynamic considerations |last1= Margolis |first1= Marvin |last2= Parker |first2= Philip |journal= Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association |year= 1972 |doi= 10.1177/000306517202000304 |volume= 20 |issue= 3 |pages= 494–511 |pmid= 4116100|s2cid= 45301787 }} Across much of Central and Eastern Europe it is believed that storks bring harmony to a family on whose property they nest.{{cite encyclopedia |url= http://www.encyclopediaofukraine.com/pages/S/T/Stork.htm |title= Stork |encyclopedia= Encyclopedia of Ukraine |publisher= Canadian Institute of Ukrainian Studies |access-date=5 December 2010}} The white stork is a popular [[motif (visual arts)|motif]] on [[postage stamp]]s, and it is featured on more than 120 stamps issued by more than 60 stamp-issuing entities.{{cite web |url= http://www.birdtheme.org/mainlyimages/index.php?spec=1432 |title= White Stork |last= Scharning |first= Kjell |work= Theme Birds on Stamps |access-date= 5 December 2010}} It is the [[List of national birds|national bird]] of Lithuania, Belarus{{cite web |url= http://en.strasbourg-europe.eu/lithuania,13812,en.html |title= Lithuania |work= Understanding the European Union: Member States |publisher= Centre d'Information sur les Institutions Européennes (CIIE) |access-date= 5 December 2010 |archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20110720173110/http://en.strasbourg-europe.eu/lithuania,13812,en.html |archive-date= 20 July 2011 }} and Poland, and it was a Polish mascot at the [[Expo 2000]] Fair in Hanover.{{cite web| title= Kingdom of Storks |work= PAIIZ Newsletter |issue= 14 |date= 22 March 2007 |url= http://www.paiz.gov.pl/index/?id=845f3cb43a07259b2e4724dfa5c5c0d1#71| publisher= Polish Information and Foreign Investment Agency (PAIIZ) |access-date= 18 March 2011}} Storks nesting in Polish villages such as [[Żywkowo, Warmian-Masurian Voivodeship|Żywkowo]] have made them tourist attractions, drawing 2000–5000 visitors a year in 2014.{{Cite journal|last1=Czajkowski|first1=Mikołaj|last2=Giergiczny|first2=Marek|last3=Kronenberg|first3=Jakub |last4=Tryjanowski|first4=Piotr|date=2014 |title=The economic recreational value of a white stork nesting colony: A case of 'stork village' in Poland|journal=Tourism Management|volume=40|pages=352–360 |doi=10.1016/j.tourman.2013.07.009}} In the 19th century, storks were also thought to only live in countries having a republican form of government.{{cite book |page= 260 |url= https://archive.org/stream/naturalhistorylo00hulmuoft#page/260/mode/1up/ |title= Natural History Lore and Legend |last=Hulme |first= F. Edward |year= 1895 |publisher= Bernard Quaritch |place= London |via= The [[Internet Archive]] |quote= The extraordinary idea that storks were found only in countries having a republican form of government held its ground for a considerable time, though it would appear as though nothing could have been simpler than its prompt disproof.}} Polish poet [[Cyprian Kamil Norwid]] mentioned storks in his poem ''Moja piosnka (II)'' ("My Song (II)"):{{cite web |title= My Song (II) |url= http://www.mission.net/poland/warsaw/literature/poems/mysong.htm |last= Norwid |first= Cyprian Kamil |work= Polish Literature |publisher= LDS Mission Network |access-date= 23 December 2010 |archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20110517032509/http://www.mission.net/poland/warsaw/literature/poems/mysong.htm |archive-date= 17 May 2011 }} {{poemquote| For the land where it's a great travesty To harm a stork's nest in a pear tree, For storks serve us all ... I am homesick, Lord! ... |translated by [[Walter Whipple]]}} [[File:Fieseler Fi 156 Storch D-IKVN.jpg|thumb|The [[Fieseler Fi 156 Storch]] received its nickname from its [[landing gear]], resembling two long legs, and its flight style.{{cite web |last1=Antón |first1=Jacinto |title=Fieseler, el aviador que trajo a la 'Cigüeña' |url=https://elpais.com/cultura/2020-12-01/fieseler-el-aviador-que-trajo-a-la-ciguena.html |website=El País |access-date=14 October 2024 |language=es |date=2 December 2020 |quote=Fieseler lo bautizó Cigüeña por su tren de aterrizaje, que parece unas patas largas, y su forma de volar. Holden sugiere que Fieseler quiso homenajear al pionero de la aviación Otto Lilienthal, que había estudiado cigüeñas en el Báltico.}}|alt=Black-and-white photo of a small propeller airplane, seen from below while climbing. Its landing gear is long and not foldable. It sports a Nazi Germany tail decoration and the sign D-IKVN.]] In 1942, [[Heinrich Himmler]] sought to use storks to carry Nazi propaganda leaflets so as to win support from the [[Boer]]s in South Africa. The idea for this "Storchbein-Propaganda" plan was a secret that was transmitted by [[Walter Schellenberg]] to be examined by the German ornithologist [[Ernst Schüz]] at the [[Rossitten Bird Observatory|Rossiten bird observatory]], who pointed out that the probability of finding marked storks in Africa was less than one percent, requiring 1000 birds to transmit 10 leaflets successfully. The plan was then dropped.{{cite news|url=http://magazin.spiegel.de/EpubDelivery/spiegel/pdf/13687293|title=Flugblätter für Afrika|date=10 January 1994|newspaper=Der Spiegel|access-date=June 30, 2018|language=de|pages=53–54|archive-date=30 June 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180630130550/http://magazin.spiegel.de/EpubDelivery/spiegel/pdf/13687293}} === Storks and delivery of babies=== [[File:Carl Spitzweg - Der Klapperstorch.jpg|thumb|upright=0.6|''Der Klapperstorch'' (''The Stork''), a painting by [[Carl Spitzweg]] (1808–1885)]] According to [[European folklore]], the stork is responsible for bringing babies to new parents. The legend is very ancient, but was popularised by a 19th-century [[Hans Christian Andersen]] story called "The Storks".{{cite book |last= Sax |first= Boria |title= The Mythical Zoo |year= 2001 |location= Oxford, UK |publisher= ABC-CLIO |isbn= 978-1-57607-612-5 |pages= 153–4}} German folklore held that storks found babies in caves or marshes and brought them to households in a basket on their backs or held in their beaks. These caves contained ''adebarsteine'' or "stork stones". The babies would then be given to the mother or dropped down the chimney. Households would notify when they wanted children by placing sweets for the stork on the window sill. From there the folklore has spread around the world to the [[Philippines]] and countries in [[South America]]. Birthmarks on the back of the head of newborn baby, ''[[nevus flammeus nuchae]]'', are sometimes referred to as stork-bite.{{cite journal |title= The incidence of birthmarks in the neonate |last1= Jacobs| first1= Alvin H. |first2= Robert G. |last2= Walton | journal= Pediatrics |year= 1976 | volume= 58 | issue=2 |pages= 218–22|doi= 10.1542/peds.58.2.218|pmid= 951136|s2cid= 245028603}} In [[Slavic mythology|Slavic mythology and pagan religion]], storks were thought to carry unborn [[Soul (spirit)|souls]] from [[Vyraj]] to Earth in spring and summer.{{cite book |last=Gieysztor |first=A. |author-link=Aleksander Gieysztor |year=1982 |language=pl |title=Mitologia Słowian |publisher=Wydawnictwa Artystyczne i Filmowe |location=Warsaw |isbn=978-83-221-0152-0}} This belief persists in the modern folk culture of many Slavic countries, in the simplified child story that "storks bring children into the world".{{cite web |last=Jakubiec |first=Z. |url=http://bocianopedia.pl/bociany-i-bocki/bocian-i-czlowiek-czyli-nasz-powszechnie-lubiany-sasiad/dlaczego-bocian-przynosil-dzieci/157 |title=Dlaczego bocian przynosił dzieci? |year=2009 |work=Bocianopedia |access-date=10 March 2011 |language=pl}} Storks were seen by [[Early Slavs]] as bringing luck, and killing one would bring misfortune.{{cite journal |last=Szczepanowicz |first=B. |title=Ptaki Ziemi Świętej: Bocian, czapla, ibis |trans-title=Birds in the Holy Land: Stork, heron, ibis |journal=Ziemia Święta |volume=rok XI 1 |issue=41 |year=2005 |url=http://basia.tomp.pl/index.php?option=com_content&task=view&id=37&Itemid=12 |language=pl |access-date=8 February 2011}} Likewise, in [[Norse mythology]], the god [[Hœnir]], responsible for giving reason to the first humans, [[Ask and Embla]], has been connected with the stork through his epithets ''long-legs'' and ''mud-king'', along with [[Indo-European languages|Indo-European]] [[cognates]] such as [[Ancient Greek|Greek]] [[wiktionary:κύκνος|κύκνος]] 'swan' and [[Sanskrit]] [[wiktionary:शकुन|शकुन]].{{cite book |author=Rydberg, V. |year=1886 |title=Investigations into Germanic Mythology, Part One |publisher= |place= |page=552}} A long-term study that showed a [[spurious correlation]] between the numbers of stork nests and human births is widely used in the teaching of basic [[statistics]] as an example to highlight that [[Correlation and dependence|correlation]] does not necessarily indicate [[Causality|causation]].{{cite journal |last1=Matthews |first1=R. |title=Storks deliver babies |journal=Teaching Statistics |volume=22 |issue=2 |pages=36–38 |year=2000 |doi=10.1111/1467-9639.00013 |s2cid=120711468 |url=http://www.uni-marburg.de/fb21/motologie/mitarbeiter_seiten/ls/storks.pdf}}{{cite book |chapter=Statistical Causality |last=Didelez |first=V. |editor-last=Østreng |editor-first=W. |editor-link=Willy Østreng |pages= 114–120 |title= Consilience: Interdisciplinary Communications 2005/2996 |year=2007 |isbn=978-82-996367-4-2 |publisher=Centre for Advanced Study |place=Oslo |chapter-url=http://www.stats.bris.ac.uk/~maxvd/Consilience_Did.pdf}} Psychoanalyst Marvin Margolis suggested that the enduring nature of the stork fable of the newborn is linked to its addressing a psychological need, in that it allays the discomfort of discussing sex and procreation with children. Birds have long been associated with the maternal symbols from pagan goddesses, such as [[Juno (mythology)|Juno]], to the [[Holy Spirit in Christianity|Holy Spirit]], and the stork may have been chosen for its white plumage depicting purity, size, and flight at high altitude likened to flying between Earth and Heaven. The fable and its relation to the internal world of the child have been discussed by [[Sigmund Freud]] and [[Carl Jung]].{{cite journal |last=Jung |first=C. |author-link=Carl Jung |year=1910 |title=The Association Method – Lecture III: Experiences concerning the psychic life of the child |journal= American Journal of Psychology |volume=31 |pages= 219–269 |jstor=1422691 |issue=3 |doi=10.2307/1413002 |hdl=11858/00-001M-0000-002B-AD55-2 |hdl-access=free}} In fact, Jung recalled being told the story himself upon the birth of his own sister.{{cite web |last= Pickles |first=P. |year=2011 |title=Jung the Man |publisher=The Society of Analytical Psychology |work=The Society of Analytical Psychology: Jungian Analysis and Psychotherapy |location=London |url=http://www.thesap.org.uk/jung-the-man |access-date=2 March 2011 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20111103203801/http://www.thesap.org.uk/jung-the-man |archive-date=3 November 2011}} The traditional link with the newborn continues with their use in [[advertising]] for such products as [[diaper|nappies]] and [[baby announcement]]s. There were also negative aspects to stork folklore; a Polish folk tale relates how God made the stork's plumage white, while the Devil gave it black wings, imbuing it with both good and evil impulses. They were also associated with handicapped or stillborn babies in Germany, explained as the stork having dropped the baby en route to the household, or as revenge or punishment for past wrongdoing. A mother who was confined to bed around the time of childbirth was said to have been "bitten" by the stork. In Denmark, storks were said to toss a nestling off the nest and then an egg in successive years. In medieval England, storks were also associated with adultery, possibly inspired by their courtship rituals. Their preening and posture saw them linked with the attribute of self-conceit.{{cite book |last=De Vries |first=A. |title=Dictionary of Symbols and Imagery |year= 1976 |page=445 |publisher=North-Holland Publishing Company |location= Amsterdam |isbn=978-0-7204-8021-4 |url=https://archive.org/details/dictionaryofsymb0000vrie/page/445}} Children of [[African American]] [[slavery in the US|slaves]] were sometimes told that storks brought white babies, while black babies were born from [[buzzard]] eggs.{{cite book |title=The White Image in the Black Mind: African-American Ideas about White People, 1830–1925 |last=Bay |first=M. |year=2000 |publisher=[[Oxford University Press]] |location=New York |isbn=978-0-19-513279-3 |page=120 白鹳（學名：Ciconia ciconia）又名红嘴白鹳、欧洲白鹳或西方白鹳，是鹳科鹳属的一种大型涉禽，主要生活在中欧和南欧、非洲西北部和亚洲西南部。白鹳是候鸟，冬季迁徙到非洲和印度热带地区和，可以一直达到南非的南部。


特征
白鹳为大型鸟类，体长100-125厘米，翼展155-215厘米，体重2.3-4.5千克。除了翅膀外，全身羽毛均为白色，喙部和腿部为红色，幼鸟则为黑色。一般在地面上缓慢而稳重地行走。飞行时将脖子伸展。


分类学
已知共有两个亚种：

指名亚种 Ciconia ciconia ciconia Linnaeus, 1758.
中亚亚种 Ciconia ciconia asiatica Severtsov, 1873.
东方白鹳（Ciconia boyciana）过去也被认为是白鹳的一个亚种，现在独立为一种。


文化
白鹳是立陶宛和白俄罗斯的国鸟，还是2000年德国汉诺威世博会波兰馆的吉祥物。
如果不受人为干扰，白鹳几乎对人类没有恐惧感。在欧洲，它们经常在人家的屋檐上筑巢。波兰人、立陶宛人和乌克兰人认为，白鹳的筑巢能为家庭带来和谐的气氛。
中世紀日耳曼北部流傳，人類未出生的靈魂會棲息於水泉、池塘、沼澤等水域，歐洲白鸛將這些幼兒靈魂帶回來，送給期待嬰兒出生的父母。因傳說與歐洲白鸛的生態習性相當吻合，所以北歐人認為嬰兒是由歐洲白鸛送給人類的。另外，十九世紀中葉的安徒生童話在故事「鸛鳥」中，也描述未出生的孩子躺在水池旁做夢，等待鸛鳥飛來，送他們去父母親的家。
白鹳也是邮票上的流行图案，世界范围内至少有120多种关于白鹳的邮票。
白鹳，喜在樹頂或高台上築巢，一次產卵約4-5枚，會將新生雛鳥中體型瘦弱者(但也常有例外)，予以淘汰，或啄死，或叼趕出巢外，任其墜落地面。
